def: Fraction of dose reaching systemic circulation. Oral bioavailability affected by absorption, metabolism, first-pass effect. Example: oral vancomycin 0%, amoxicillin 90%.
Drug metabolite with pharmacologic activity. Examples: morphine-6-glucuronide, enalaprilat. Can prolong drug effects and require monitoring.
Energy-dependent carrier movement against concentration gradient. Examples: P-glycoprotein, OAT, OCT. Subject to saturation and interactions.
Method to predict human dosing from animal studies using body weight. Formula: Dose_human = Dose_animal × (BW_human/BW_animal)^0.7.
Theoretical volume into which drug distributes: Vd = Dose/Cp₀. Highly lipophilic drugs have large Vd; protein-bound drugs have low Vd.
Integral of drug concentration over time. Proportional to absorbed dose. AUC = Dose/CL. Used for bioavailability assessment and TDM.
Fraction of administered dose reaching systemic circulation. IV=100%; oral typically 50-90%. Affected by absorption, stability, first-pass metabolism.
Elimination via liver into bile and feces. Occurs for high MW compounds (>300 Da) and lipophilic drugs. Can prolong half-life via enterohepatic recirculation.
Maximum plasma concentration after oral/IM administration at time Tmax. Critical for determining time vs concentration-dependent killing.
Volume of plasma completely cleared of drug per unit time. CL = Vd × Ke. Determines Css = (Dose/τ)/CL. Kidney + liver are major routes.
Estimates CrCl: [(140-age)×BW×0.85(if female)]/(72×SCr). Used for renal dose adjustment. Less accurate in extremes of age/weight.
Mathematical model: one-compartment (rapid equilibration); two-compartment (central + peripheral). Explains biphasic plasma curves.
Graph showing absorption, distribution, elimination phases. Determines Cmax, Tmax, AUC, elimination rate constant.
Measure of renal function. Normal 90-120 mL/min; <30 indicates significant impairment. Used for renal dose adjustments.
Minimum concentration before next dose. Must exceed MEC for sustained efficacy. Low Cmin = subtherapeutic.
Initial phase where concentration drops rapidly due to distribution from central to peripheral compartments. Two-compartment kinetics show biphasic curve.
Relationship between dose and pharmacologic response. Sigmoidal curves in Emax model. Defines EC50 and therapeutic window.
Removal of active drug via metabolism or excretion. First-order (concentration-dependent); zero-order (capacity-limited).
Drug concentration producing 50% maximal effect. Lower EC50 = higher potency. Measured in vitro.
Time for concentration to decrease 50%. t½ = 0.693/Ke. After 5 half-lives, reach 97% steady state.
First-order rate constant: fraction eliminated per unit time. Ke = CL/Vd. t½ = 0.693/Ke.
Pharmacodynamic model: Effect = Emax × C/(EC50 + C). Sigmoidal curve for dose-response.
Bile-excreted drugs reabsorbed in small intestine, returning to liver. Prolongs half-life. Interrupted by cholestyramine.
Increased expression of metabolizing enzymes. Rifampin, phenytoin induce CYP3A4. Increases metabolism of substrate drugs.
Decreased enzyme activity. Ketoconazole inhibits CYP3A4. Increases plasma concentration of substrate drugs.
Fraction of drug removed during single organ pass. ER = CLorgan/Q. High ER drugs sensitive to blood flow changes.
Elimination rate proportional to concentration. Most drugs. Constant half-life. Linear semi-log decline.
Metabolism before systemic circulation, reducing bioavailability. Nitroglycerin, propranolol require sublingual/IV dosing.
Percentage unbound in plasma. Only free drug crosses membranes. Highly bound drugs (>90%) may be displaced.
Passive filtration through glomerulus of small MW drugs (<5000 Da). Normal 90-120 mL/min. Primary renal route.
Time for concentration to decrease 50%. Determines dosing interval. t½ = 0.693 × Vd/CL. Independent of dose.
Fraction of liver blood flow removing drug. CLh = Q × ER. High extraction drugs sensitive to flow changes.
ER >0.7: liver removes 70%+ per pass. Clearance depends on blood flow, not enzyme activity. Nitroglycerin, propranolol, lidocaine.
Ligand-receptor binding model: Effect = [C^n]/(EC50^n + [C^n]). Hill coefficient describes cooperativity.
Measure of inhibitor affinity for enzyme. Lower Ki = stronger inhibition. Predicts drug interaction potential.
Initial dose to rapidly achieve Css: LD = Vd × Css_target. Followed by maintenance doses.
ER <0.3: liver removes <30% per pass. Clearance depends on enzyme activity. Warfarin, theophylline, diazepam.
Regular dose to maintain Css: MD = Css × CL × τ/F. Frequency depends on half-life.
Enzyme kinetics: V = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]). Becomes zero-order at saturation.
Elimination NOT proportional to concentration. Half-life varies with dose. Phenytoin, salicylates, theophylline.
Assumes rapid equilibration between plasma and tissues. Monoexponential decline. Simpler than two-compartment.
Fraction of oral dose reaching systemic circulation. Reduced by first-pass, poor absorption. Typical 5-90%.
ATP-dependent pump removing drugs from cells. Reduces absorption, increases biliary/renal excretion. Substrate for digoxin, cyclosporine.
Non-energy dependent movement down concentration gradient. Lipophilic unionized drugs cross readily. No saturation.
Ratio of Cmax to Cmin. Important for time-dependent (prefer lower) vs concentration-dependent killing (can tolerate higher).
Study of drug effects: mechanism of action, dose-response, efficacy, potency, adverse effects.
How genetic variation affects drug response and metabolism. CYP2D6 polymorphisms, TPMT mutations, VKORC1.
Drug movement through body: absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME). Determines plasma concentration vs time.
Reversible binding to albumin, alpha-1 glycoprotein. Highly bound (>90%) = lower free fraction. Displacement interactions.
Inactive drug metabolized to active form. Enalapril → enalaprilat, clopidogrel → active metabolite, codeine → morphine.
Elimination via kidneys: filtration + secretion - reabsorption. CLr = (U_drug × V)/P_drug. Proportional to GFR.
Nonlinear kinetics when drug saturates metabolic enzymes. Vmax reached; zero-order kinetics results.
Input = output; constant plasma concentration. Reached ~5 half-lives. Css = (F × Dose/τ)/CL.
Measure plasma concentration to optimize therapy. Especially narrow TI drugs (digoxin, vancomycin). Sample at Cmin.
Ratio of toxic to effective dose: TI = TD50/ED50. Narrow TI = requires TDM. Wide TI = greater safety margin.
Bacterial killing depends on TIME above MIC, not peak. Beta-lactams, glycopeptides. Frequent dosing maximizes time-above-MIC.
~5 half-lives regardless of dose. After 3 half-lives = 87.5% Css; after 5 = 96.9%.
Passive reabsorption from renal tubule back into blood. Reduces urinary excretion. pH-dependent for weak acids/bases.
Active transport from blood into renal tubule lumen. OAT/OCT involved. Probenecid blocks penicillin secretion.
Central (plasma, highly perfused) + peripheral (fat, poorly perfused) compartments. Biphasic plasma curve.
Theoretical volume drug distributes into: Vd = Dose/Cp₀. Does NOT represent true tissue volume. Used for loading dose calculation.
Elimination independent of concentration, occurring at saturation. Constant amount/time eliminated. Phenytoin, ethanol, aspirin.
Drug binding receptor producing cellular response. Full agonists: Emax = 100%; partial agonists: Emax <100%.
GPCR mediating vasoconstriction, inotropy, mydriasis. Blocked by prazosin, doxazosin. Agonized by norepinephrine.
Inhibitory GPCR causing vasoconstriction, decreased NE release (negative feedback), CNS sedation. Clonidine agonist.
Drug binding receptor with NO cellular response, blocking agonist. Competitive (reversible) or noncompetitive (irreversible).
Muscarinic blockade: dry mouth, mydriasis, urinary retention, constipation, tachycardia. Common with antihistamines, antidepressants, antipsychotics.
Substance countering toxin/overdose effects. Naloxone (opioid), fomepizole (ethylene glycol), acetylcysteine (acetaminophen).
Sympathetic (adrenergic, fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic (cholinergic, rest-digest) divisions.
GPCR on heart/kidney mediating increased HR, contractility, AV conduction, renin release. Blocked by beta-blockers.
GPCR on bronchial/vascular smooth muscle mediating bronchodilation. Agonized by albuterol. Target for asthma treatment.
Maximum response that cannot increase with further dose. Aspirin/NSAID analgesia shows a ceiling, whereas anti-inflammatory effect continues at higher doses.
Muscarinic/nicotinic activation: SLUDGE (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI, emesis), miosis, bradycardia, bronchoconstriction.
Reversible enzyme inhibition with competing substrate. Overcome by increasing substrate. Km increases, Vmax unchanged.
Bacterial killing depends on PEAK concentration vs MIC. Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones. Once-daily dosing.
CYP450 metabolizes theophylline, caffeine, warfarin. Induced by smoking, charbroiled foods; inhibited by ciprofloxacin. ~5% hepatic metabolism.
CYP450 metabolizes clopidogrel (Plavix), omeprazole, phenytoin, diazepam. Loss-of-function variants impair clopidogrel activation. ~10% metabolism.
CYP450 metabolizes warfarin, NSAIDs, phenytoin. Variants (CYP2C9*2, *3) slow metabolism, increase warfarin toxicity. ~11% hepatic metabolism.
CYP450 metabolizes codeine, tamoxifen, antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics. Creates ultrarapid/slow metabolizers. PGx testing guides dosing. Inhibited by SSRIs.
Most abundant CYP450, metabolizes ~50% drugs: statins, protease inhibitors, macrolides, CCBs. Induced by rifampin; inhibited by ketoconazole, ritonavir. Major interaction source.
Alteration of drug effect by another substance via pharmacokinetic (metabolism, excretion) or pharmacodynamic (additive, synergistic) mechanisms.
Reduced response to repeated dose, requiring escalation. Mechanisms: receptor desensitization, decreased density, increased metabolism.
Maximum response achievable regardless of dose. High efficacy = full effect. Intrinsic activity concept. Distinguished from potency.
Drug with intrinsic activity = 1.0, producing maximal response (Emax = 100%). Examples: isoproterenol, morphine.
Unpredictable, unusual reaction genetically determined, non-dose related. Examples: isoniazid lupus, carbamazepine hypersensitivity.
Drug producing response below basal level by reducing constitutive receptor activity. Timolol reduces resting HR below normal.
Covalent enzyme inhibition, permanent. Vmax decreases until new enzyme synthesized. Aspirin irreversibly acetylates COX.
Irreversible inhibition where enzyme converts inhibitor to reactive intermediate covalently binding enzyme. Examples: erythromycin/clarithromycin, diltiazem, verapamil, ritonavir.
Drug with intrinsic activity 0 < α < 1.0, producing submaximal response (Emax <100%). Can act as agonist or antagonist. Pindolol, buprenorphine.
Genetic basis of drug response. CYP450 variants, TPMT, VKORC1, HLA-B*5701. Enables precision dosing.
Amount needed to produce 50% maximal effect. Low EC50 = high potency. In vitro measurement. Does NOT indicate efficacy.
Early model: response proportional to receptor occupancy. Modified by allosteric, biased signaling. Still useful.
Ability to interact with one receptor vs others. Higher selectivity = fewer off-target effects. Metoprolol β1-selective vs propranolol nonselective.
Life-threatening toxidrome from excessive serotonergic activity: agitation, confusion, hyperthermia, hyperreflexia, clonus, rigidity. SSRIs + MAOIs. Treat with benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine.
Rapid loss of effect with repeated dose (minutes-hours), unlike tolerance (days-weeks). Ephedrine tachyphylaxis from NE depletion.
Plasma concentration range between MEC and MTC. Narrow window = requires TDM. Wide window = greater safety.
Enzyme inhibition increasing over time via mechanism-based inactivation. Example: erythromycin/clarithromycin TDI of CYP3A4.
Dose-related, predictable adverse reaction from exaggerated pharmacologic effect. Preventable by dose reduction. Majority of ADRs.
Non-dose related, unpredictable adverse reaction (immunologic, idiosyncratic, genetic). Not preventable by dose reduction. Stevens-Johnson, anaphylaxis.
Increased expression of receptors, enzymes, transporters in response to decreased activity. Long-term antagonist = more receptors.
Decreased expression in response to persistent agonist/increased activity. Continuous beta-agonist = fewer β-receptors. Causes tolerance.
Phase II enzyme catalyzing glucuronidation (acetaminophen, morphine, bilirubin). Variants (UGT1A1*28) impair glucuronidation. Gilbert syndrome.
Resistance against ventricular ejection. Determined by SVR, aortic valve resistance, wall stress. Increased by HTN; decreased by vasodilation.
Class III antiarrhythmic with all 4 Vaughan-Williams properties. Most effective SVT/VT. Toxicity: pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid, hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation.
Blocks ACE preventing angiotensin II formation. Vasodilation, reduced aldosterone, RAAS inhibition. Cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema side effects.
Selectively blocks AT1 receptors. Similar to ACEi without cough. Hyperkalemia, angioedema rare. Often combined with ACEi (controversial).
ARB + neprilysin inhibitor (sacubitril/valsartan). Superior to ACEi in HFrEF. Increases natriuretic peptides (vasodilation, diuresis).
Acquired thrombophilia from antiphospholipid antibodies. Recurrent thrombosis, pregnancy loss. Anticoagulation (warfarin INR 2-3).
Aortic valve narrowing limiting cardiac output. Rheumatic, bicuspid, degenerative etiologies. Syncope, dyspnea, angina. Sudden cardiac death risk.
Most common arrhythmia: disorganized atrial activity, irregular VR. Stroke risk (CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥2 → anticoagulation). Heart failure, syncope.
Regular rapid atrium (250-350 bpm), sawtooth ECG. Often paroxysmal. Palpitations, dyspnea. May degenerate to AFib.
Conducts impulses atria→ventricles with 100-120 ms physiologic delay. Target for rate control (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin). Dysfunction → AV block.
Blocks beta-adrenergic receptors reducing HR, contractility, BP. Cardioselective (metoprolol, atenolol) vs nonselective (propranolol). HTN, angina, MI, arrhythmias, HF.
Hormone from ventricles in volume overload. Vasodilation, diuresis, RAAS inhibition. Elevated in HF. NT-proBNP more stable for diagnostics.
Genetic channelopathy (SCN5A mutation) predisposing to SCD from polymorphic VT. Coved ST elevation V1-V2. ICD treatment; avoid fever, sodium channel blockers.
Blocks L-type calcium channels reducing contractility/AV conduction (diltiazem, verapamil) or vascular (dihydropyridines). HTN, angina, SVT. Edema, constipation.
Myocardial injury indicators: troponins (high sensitivity), BNP/NT-proBNP, CK-MB. Troponins most specific for MI; elevated in HF, sepsis, renal failure.
Blood pumped per minute: CO = HR × SV. Normal 4-6 L/min. Determined by preload, afterload, contractility (Frank-Starling law).
Pericardial fluid accumulation compressing heart, preventing filling. Beck triad: hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds. Treatment: pericardiocentesis.
Inadequate CO causing tissue hypoperfusion/organ dysfunction. From acute MI, HF decompensation, severe arrhythmia. Inotropes, vasopressors, mechanical support.
Heart muscle disease: dilated (reduced EF), hypertrophic (LV thickening), restrictive (stiff), takotsubo. Viral, ischemic, genetic etiologies cause HF.
Synchronized electrical current to convert arrhythmias (AFib, VT, SVT) to sinus rhythm. Requires sedation. 100-200 J synchronized; 200-360 J unsynchronized VF.
Stroke risk in AFib: C=CHF, H=HTN, A=age≥75 (2 pts), D=DM, S=stroke (2 pts), V=vascular, A=age 65-74, Sc=sex female. Anticoagulation recommended at score ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women.
Effect on HR. Positive (increase HR): sympathomimetics, beta-agonists, atropine. Negative (decrease HR): beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, adenosine.
HF state where CO maintained via compensation without acute decompensation symptoms. Stable but risk for decompensation.
Specialized tissue: SA node (pacemaker, 60-100 bpm) → atrium → AV node → His bundle → Purkinje fibers → ventricles. Blocks cause heart block.
Atherosclerotic coronary narrowing reducing myocardial perfusion. Angina, MI, SCD. Risk factors: HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking.
Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, prasugrel, ticagrelor) post-ACS/PCI. Duration 12 months ACS, 6-12 months post-PCI. Balances stent thrombosis vs bleeding.
Impaired LV relaxation/filling despite preserved EF (≥50%). Elevated filling pressures, pulmonary congestion. From HTN, LVH, restrictive cardiomyopathy.
Cardiac glycoside: inotropic (increases contractility), chronotropic (decreases HR), vagomimetic. Narrow TI (0.5-2 ng/mL). AFib rate control, systolic HF. Toxicity: arrhythmias.
Factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban) or thrombin inhibitor (dabigatran). Rapid onset, no INR monitoring. AFib, VTE, thromboprophylaxis.
Effect on AV nodal conduction velocity. Positive (increase conduction): sympathomimetics, atropine. Negative (decrease): beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, adenosine.
Percentage of EDV ejected: EF = (EDV - ESV)/EDV × 100%. Normal >50%. EF <40% = systolic dysfunction; HFrEF <40%.
Endocardium/valve infection (Streptococci, Staphylococci, HACEK). Fever, new murmur, emboli. Diagnosed: blood cultures + echo. Prolonged IV antibiotics.
Direct Factor Xa inhibitors: apixaban (Eliquis), rivaroxaban (Xarelto), edoxaban (Savaysa). Oral, fixed-dose, no monitoring. Rapid onset/offset.
Clot dissolution via plasmin activation. Plasminogen → plasmin → fibrin degradation. tPA, streptokinase, urokinase for acute MI/stroke.
CO increases with ventricular preload (stretch) until mechanical disadvantage. Early HF compensation; flat in decompensated HF.
Standard systolic HF therapy: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA. SGLT2 inhibitors added. Reduces mortality, hospitalizations.
HF with EF ≥50%, from diastolic dysfunction. Control HTN, rate control, diuretics. Fewer evidence-based therapies than HFrEF.
HF with EF <40% from systolic dysfunction. Responsive to GDMT (ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blockers, MRA, SGLT2i). Improves survival, reduces hospitalizations.
Assessment of cardiovascular function: HR, BP, CVP, CO, SVR. ICU via Swan-Ganz (CO, SVR, PCWP), arterial line. Guides fluid management, vasopressors.
Blood clotting: primary (platelets), secondary (coagulation cascade → fibrin), tertiary (fibrinolysis). Disorders: thrombophilia or coagulopathy.
Heart failure with mildly reduced EF 41-49%. Recently defined. Prognosis intermediate between HFpEF/HFrEF. GDMT guidelines evolving.
Improved sensitivity/NPV for MI rule-out vs troponin. Detects smaller myocardial injury. Better early MI detection; false positives increased.
Genetic disorder (sarcomeric protein mutations) causing unexplained LV hypertrophy. Sudden cardiac death, AFib, HF risk. Murmur increases with Valsalva. Beta-blockers.
Effect on cardiac contractility. Positive inotrope (increase force): catecholamines, inotropes, digoxin. Negative inotrope (decrease): beta-blockers, CCBs.
Standardized warfarin measure: INR = (PT_patient/PT_normal)^ISI. Target 2-3 most indications. Requires monitoring; affected by food/drugs.
Beta-blocker property (pindolol, acebutolol) partially activating β-receptors at rest. Less bradycardia, less vasoconstriction.
Percentage of LV EDV ejected. Normal >50%. EF ≤40% = systolic dysfunction. Measured by echo. Prognostic HF marker.
Heparin fragments (enoxaparin, dalteparin): predictable PK, longer half-life (5 hrs), once-daily/BID dosing. VTE prophylaxis, treatment, ACS. Renal elimination; avoid severe renal failure.
Genetic or acquired QT prolongation (>460 ms female, >450 ms male). Torsades de pointes risk. Antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics, macrolides prolong QT.
Effect on diastolic function/myocardial relaxation. Positive lusitropic: beta-agonists, PDE inhibitors improve relaxation. Negative: ischemia, restrictive disease.
Average arterial pressure: MAP ≈ DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP). Normal 70-100 mmHg. MAP <65 indicates inadequate perfusion.
Composite endpoint in trials: death, MI, stroke, revascularization. Statins reduce MACE by ~20-30% in high-risk.
Acute myocardial necrosis from coronary occlusion. STEMI: ST elevation (transmural); NSTEMI: no ST elevation (subendocardial). Chest pain, dyspnea. PCI/thrombolytics, dual antiplatelet.
Spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) reduces sodium retention, sympathetic activation. Improves HF outcomes. Hyperkalemia risk; monitor potassium.
N-terminal pro-BNP, more stable than BNP with longer half-life. Elevated in HF, cardiomyopathy, MI. HF diagnosis, prognosis, therapy titration.

HF symptom classification: Class I = no symptoms/no limitation, II = symptoms with ordinary activity, III = symptoms with less-than-ordinary activity (marked limitation), IV = symptoms at rest. Guides therapy intensity.
Antiplatelet: clopidogrel (Plavix), prasugrel (Effient), ticagrelor (Brilinta). Block ADP P2Y12 receptor on platelets. DAPT post-ACS/PCI.
Implantable device sensing intrinsic activity, pacing when rate drops below threshold. Indications: bradycardia, AV block, sick sinus, cardiomyopathy.
Sudden-onset supraventricular tachycardia (150-250 bpm) from AV nodal reentry (AVNRT) or accessory pathway (AVRT). Palpitations, syncope. Vagal maneuvers, adenosine, ablation.

Monoclonal antibodies (evolocumab, alirocumab) inhibiting PCSK9, decreasing LDL receptor degradation to increase LDL receptor recycling. LDL reduction 50-60%. Familial hypercholesterolemia, CAD. High cost.
Catheter-based coronary revascularization: balloon angioplasty ± stent. Preferred STEMI reperfusion <120 min. Followed by DAPT.
Ventricular EDV/pressure representing myocardial fiber stretch before contraction. Increased by volume loading; decreased by diuresis. Determines CO via Frank-Starling.
Prothrombin time measures factors II, VII, X (extrinsic pathway). Reported as INR for warfarin monitoring. PT prolongation: warfarin, factor deficiency, liver disease.
ECG interval representing ventricular depolarization (0.08-0.12 sec). Wide QRS (>0.12 sec) suggests bundle branch block or ventricular origin. QRS amplitude = LVH.
ECG Q wave to T wave end representing ventricular depolarization + repolarization (0.36-0.44 sec). Prolongation risks torsades. Corrected for HR: QTc.
QT corrected for HR using Bazett: QTc = QT/√RR. Normal <450 ms (men) or <460 ms (women). QTc >500 ms significant prolongation risk.
Cascade regulating BP/fluid balance: renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → (ACE) → angiotensin II → aldosterone. Inhibited by ACEi, ARB, MRA.
Enzyme from juxtaglomerular cells responding to low renal perfusion, initiating RAAS. Directly measured for HTN diagnosis. Beta-blockers reduce renin.
Biventricular pacemaker for symptomatic systolic HF with QRS >120 ms. Improves synchrony between LV/RV. ~20% mortality reduction.
Muscle necrosis releasing myoglobin (dark urine), CK (>1000 U/L), electrolytes. Statins + fibrates, muscle injury. Complications: AKI, hyperkalemia, DIC.
RV dysfunction impairing blood forward flow into pulmonary circulation. Chronic lung disease (cor pulmonale), RV MI, pulmonary HTN. Elevated JVP, edema.
Inhibits renal glucose reabsorption causing glycosuria, osmotic diuresis. Recent CV/renal benefits in HFrEF, CKD beyond glucose lowering. Empagliflozin, dapagliflozin.
Normal rhythm from SA node (60-100 bpm) with organized atrial/ventricular depolarization. Goal rhythm in arrhythmia management.
HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, simvastatin). LDL ↓30-50%, HDL ↑, MACE ↓. Myopathy, hepatotoxicity, DM risk. Major CYP3A4 interactions.
ST-elevation MI from transmural MI with coronary occlusion. ST elevation ≥1 mm ≥2 contiguous leads. Urgent reperfusion (PCI <120 min or thrombolysis).
Blood volume ejected per beat: SV = EDV - ESV. Normal ~70 mL. Determined by preload, afterload, contractility.
Systemic circulation resistance. SVR = (MAP - CVP)/CO × 80. Normal 800-1200. Elevated in shock/HTN; decreased in sepsis.
Impaired ventricular contraction resulting in reduced EF (<40%). MI, cardiomyopathy, HTN. Decreased CO, HFrEF.
Polymorphic VT with twisting QRS axis, associated with QT prolongation. Antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics, macrolides, electrolyte abnormalities. Treat: magnesium, defibrillation.
Blood clot formation in vessel (arterial or venous). Virchow triad: stasis, vessel injury, hypercoagulability. Complications: VTE, stroke, MI.
In vivo blood clot. Distinguished from embolus (detached). Platelets, fibrin, RBCs. Dissolved via fibrinolysis or anticoagulation.
ACS risk stratification predicting mortality. Incorporates age, coronary disease, troponin, CK-MB, STT changes. TIMI 0/1 low risk, TIMI 6 very high.
Cardiac regulatory proteins (troponin I, T) released from myocardial injury. High sensitivity (hs-cTn) detects smaller infarcts. Elevated 2-4 hrs, peak 24-48 hrs, persist 10-14 days.
Indirect thrombin (and factor Xa) inhibitor acting via antithrombin. Rapid IV/SC onset. Shorter half-life than LMWH. Requires aPTT monitoring. ACS, VTE, cardiopulmonary bypass.
Vessel widening reducing resistance and BP. Nitrates, ACEi, ARB, hydralazine. Decreases afterload; improves CO in cardiogenic shock.
Hormone causing vasoconstriction (V1) and water reabsorption (V2). Rescue vasopressor in refractory hypotension/cardiac arrest. Improves coronary perfusion pressure.
Rapid ventricular rhythm (>100 bpm) below AV node. Monomorphic (uniform) vs polymorphic (variable QRS). Unstable VT → immediate defibrillation.
Venous thrombosis: DVT (leg) or PE (lungs). Virchow triad pathophysiology. Prevention: compression, anticoagulation. Treatment: anticoagulation.
Vitamin K antagonist inhibiting factors II, VII, IX, X. Slow onset (3-5 days), long half-life (36-42 hrs). INR monitoring required. AFib, mechanical valves. Reversible with vitamin K, FFP, PCC.

Concentration-dependent bactericidal antibiotics (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin) that inhibit bacterial protein synthesis (30S ribosome). Exhibit significant post-antibiotic effect; dosed once-daily preferred for most indications to maximize Cmax/MIC and reduce nephrotoxicity. Monitor peak (8-10 mcg/mL) and trough (<1 mcg/mL) for traditional dosing; AUC-guided for once-daily.

Coordinated program to optimize antibiotic use to improve patient outcomes, reduce resistance, and minimize adverse effects. Core strategies include prospective audit with feedback, formulary restriction/pre-authorization, de-escalation at 48-72h based on culture data, and IV-to-PO conversion. Procalcitonin (PCT) is a validated biomarker to guide antibiotic discontinuation.

Presence of viable bacteria in the bloodstream confirmed by positive blood culture. Distinguishes from transient bacteremia (brief, clinically insignificant) vs sustained bacteremia requiring treatment. MRSA bacteremia mandates repeat cultures at 48-96h, echocardiography, and minimum 14-day course. Source control (remove infected catheter/drain abscess) is as important as antibiotics.

Largest class of antibiotics sharing a beta-lactam ring that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis (penicillin-binding proteins). Includes penicillins, cephalosporins (1st-4th gen), carbapenems, and monobactams (aztreonam). Time-dependent killing: efficacy correlates with %T>MIC. Resistance via beta-lactamase enzymes (ESBLs, KPC, MBL) is the major clinical challenge.

Bacterial enzymes that hydrolyze the beta-lactam ring, inactivating the antibiotic. Extended-spectrum beta-lactamases (ESBLs) hydrolyze 3rd-gen cephalosporins; KPC carbapenemases hydrolyze carbapenems. Beta-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanate, tazobactam, avibactam, vaborbactam) protect against specific enzyme classes and are combined with beta-lactams.

Clostridioides difficile infection causes antibiotic-associated diarrhea via toxin A/B. Risk factors: fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, broad-spectrum beta-lactams, PPIs, age >65, hospitalization. Treatment: fidaxomicin preferred over vancomycin (lower recurrence); metronidazole only for mild non-severe; bezlotoxumab (monoclonal antibody) reduces recurrence. Avoid antiperistaltics.

Outpatient no comorbidities: amoxicillin OR doxycycline OR macrolide (if local pneumococcal resistance <25%). With comorbidities: amoxicillin/clavulanate or cephalosporin + macrolide/doxycycline, OR respiratory fluoroquinolone.

Broadest spectrum beta-lactam antibiotics (meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem, doripenem). Resistant to most beta-lactamases except carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA). Definitive therapy for ESBL-producing organisms, AmpC-producing bacteria. Ertapenem lacks Pseudomonas/Acinetobacter coverage. Meropenem is preferred for CNS infections due to lower seizure risk vs imipenem.
First-line for Candida bloodstream infections and invasive candidiasis (echinocandins preferred over fluconazole for initial therapy).

Severity scoring tool for community-acquired pneumonia: Confusion, Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >19), Respiratory rate ≥30, Blood pressure <90 systolic or ≤60 diastolic, age ≥65. Score 0-1: outpatient; 2: consider admission; 3+: hospitalize/ICU evaluation. Simpler than PSI/PORT score and easier to calculate at bedside.

Cyclic lipopeptide antibiotic that depolarizes gram-positive bacterial cell membranes causing rapid cell death. Concentration-dependent killing. Active against MRSA, VRE, and vancomycin-resistant organisms. AVOID for pulmonary infections — inactivated by surfactant. Monitor CK weekly (myopathy risk); hold statins if possible. Dose: 4-6 mg/kg/day (SSTI); 6-12 mg/kg/day (bacteremia/IE).

Antibiotic stewardship practice of narrowing empiric broad-spectrum therapy to targeted narrow-spectrum agents based on culture and sensitivity results at 48-72 hours. Reduces resistance selection pressure, adverse effects (CDI, nephrotoxicity), and cost. Key principle: start broad, de-escalate early based on data, not clinical response alone.

Antibiotic treatment initiated before culture results are available, based on likely pathogens for a given clinical syndrome and local resistance patterns (antibiogram). Breadth depends on severity: septic shock warrants broadest coverage. Must cover most likely organisms while awaiting culture data (24-48h turnaround). Document planned de-escalation date in the chart.

Enzymes that hydrolyze all penicillins, cephalosporins, and monobactams. Commonly produced by E. coli and Klebsiella. Definitive treatment: meropenem or ertapenem (carbapenems). The MERINO trial established that piperacillin-tazobactam has unacceptably high failure rates for ESBL bacteremia despite in vitro susceptibility — the inoculum effect explains this (enzyme overwhelms the inhibitor at high bacterial burden).

Triazole antifungal inhibiting ergosterol synthesis (CYP51/lanosterol demethylase). First-line for Candida UTI and oropharyngeal/esophageal candidiasis. NOT active against Aspergillus, Candida krusei (intrinsically resistant), or Candida glabrata (often resistant). Strong CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitor — major interactions with warfarin (↑INR), tacrolimus, phenytoin. Oral bioavailability ~90% (IV = PO).

Pneumonia developing ≥48 hours after hospital admission. Causative organisms differ from CAP: gram-negative bacilli (Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Acinetobacter), MRSA. MRSA risk factors: prior MRSA colonization, MRSA prevalence >20%, prior antibiotics. Empiric: antipseudomonal beta-lactam ± vancomycin/linezolid if MRSA risk. De-escalate based on BAL/culture. Duration: 7 days.

Human Immunodeficiency Virus infects CD4+ T-cells leading to progressive immune deficiency (AIDS at CD4<200). ART (antiretroviral therapy) with 3-drug regimen suppresses viral replication to undetectable. Preferred 2024 regimen: bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine (Biktarvy) — INSTI backbone, high barrier to resistance, once-daily. ART should start at diagnosis regardless of CD4 count.

Phenomenon where antibiotic efficacy decreases at high bacterial density, as seen with beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations against ESBL producers. At high inocula (e.g., bacteremia), overwhelming enzyme production inactivates the inhibitor (tazobactam), leaving the beta-lactam vulnerable. This is the mechanistic explanation for the MERINO trial results showing pip-tazo failure despite in vitro susceptibility.

Oxazolidinone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis (50S ribosome, unique binding site). Active against MRSA, VRE, and drug-resistant gram-positives. Oral bioavailability ~100% (IV=PO). Serotonergic — avoid with SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk). MAO inhibitor activity — avoid tyramine-rich foods. Monitor CBC weekly (myelosuppression, thrombocytopenia). No dose adjustment needed for renal impairment.

Clinical finding from the 2018 MERINO trial that piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5g q6h) had 3x higher 30-day mortality compared to meropenem for ESBL-producing E. coli or Klebsiella bacteremia (12.3% vs 3.7%). Established that pip-tazo CANNOT be used as definitive therapy for ESBL bacteremia despite in vitro susceptibility. Meropenem or ertapenem are the only appropriate definitive agents.

S. aureus resistant to all beta-lactams via mecA gene encoding modified PBP2a with low affinity for beta-lactams. Treatment: vancomycin (AUC/MIC 400-600) or daptomycin for bacteremia/serious infections; TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin for SSTI. Healthcare-associated (HA-MRSA): multi-drug resistant; community-associated (CA-MRSA): often TMP-SMX sensitive, produces Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL).

Life-threatening invasive fungal infection by Mucorales species (Rhizopus, Mucor). Risk factors: poorly controlled DM (especially DKA), prolonged neutropenia, iron overload, deferoxamine use. Classic presentation: rhino-orbital-cerebral form (black eschar, proptosis). Uniquely resistant to voriconazole and echinocandins. Treatment: liposomal amphotericin B + aggressive surgical debridement ± isavuconazole/posaconazole step-down.

Single oral temp ≥38.3°C OR ≥38°C for ≥1h in a patient with ANC <500 cells/mcL (or expected nadir <500). Medical emergency. Empiric antipseudomonal beta-lactam within 1 hour (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem). Add vancomycin only if hemodynamically unstable, skin/catheter infection, or mucositis. MASCC score guides risk stratification for outpatient management.

Anti-pseudomonal penicillin + beta-lactamase inhibitor (4.5g q6h or q8h extended infusion). Covers gram-positives, gram-negatives including Pseudomonas, and anaerobes. Extended infusion (4-hour infusion q8h) optimizes %T>MIC for resistant organisms. Critical MERINO lesson: AVOID as definitive therapy for ESBL bacteremia. Combination with vancomycin increases AKI risk 4-fold (separate from vanco toxicity alone).

Biomarker of bacterial infection; during systemic infection procalcitonin is produced ubiquitously by parenchymal tissues (liver, lung, etc.) in response to bacterial endotoxins and inflammatory cytokines (baseline is from thyroid C-cells).

Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection (Sepsis-3 definition). Defined by SOFA score increase ≥2 points. Septic shock: vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 AND lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures ×2, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid, vasopressors for MAP <65, lactate measurement.

Sequential folate-synthesis inhibitor combination (trimethoprim = dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor; sulfamethoxazole = dihydropteroate synthase inhibitor) with synergistic bactericidal activity against many gram-positives and gram-negatives.
Duration: 7-8 days non-inferior to 14-15 days (Chastre et al. PneumA, JAMA 2003).
Active against gram-positive organisms including MRSA; NOT active against VRE (both VanA and VanB gene clusters confer vancomycin resistance — VanB retains teicoplanin susceptibility).

Enterococcus (E. faecalis, E. faecium) resistant to vancomycin via VanA or VanB gene clusters modifying the D-Ala-D-Ala target. E. faecium more resistant to most antibiotics than E. faecalis. VanA: resistant to vancomycin AND teicoplanin; VanB: resistant only to vancomycin. Treatment: linezolid or daptomycin. VRE bacteremia is associated with high mortality; combination therapy sometimes used. Strict contact precautions required.

Second-generation triazole antifungal; first-line for invasive aspergillosis (VORICONAZOLE > amphotericin B — Herbrecht 2002 trial). Also active against Candida, Fusarium, Scedosporium. NOT effective against Mucorales (Mucormycosis). Nonlinear PK due to CYP2C19 saturable metabolism; therapeutic drug monitoring (trough 1-5.5 mcg/mL) recommended. ADRs: visual disturbances (photopsia), hepatotoxicity, QTc prolongation. Major CYP inhibitor.

Pharmacodynamic target for antibiotics with AUC-dependent killing (e.g., vancomycin, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides). Ratio of total drug exposure (area under the curve) to the minimum inhibitory concentration. Vancomycin target: AUC24/MIC 400-600 mg·h/L for S. aureus (assuming MIC ≤1). Higher AUC/MIC = better bactericidal effect for these agents. Replaced trough-only monitoring for vancomycin.

Pharmacodynamic target for concentration-dependent antibiotics (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones). Ratio of peak concentration to minimum inhibitory concentration. Target for aminoglycosides: Cmax/MIC 8-10 for optimal bactericidal effect. Higher ratios improve efficacy and reduce resistance selection. Once-daily aminoglycoside dosing leverages this by achieving high peaks, then allowing drug-free trough periods.

Pharmacodynamic target for time-dependent antibiotics (beta-lactams). Percentage of dosing interval during which free drug concentration exceeds the MIC. Optimal target: bacteriostatic effect at 30-40% T>MIC; bactericidal at 50-70%. Achieved through frequent dosing, extended infusions (4-hour infusions of pip-tazo, meropenem), or continuous infusion. Critical for treating resistant organisms with elevated MICs.

ICU liberation protocol: A=Assess, prevent, manage Pain; B=Both Spontaneous Awakening Trials (SAT) and Spontaneous Breathing Trials (SBT); C=Choice of analgesia and sedation; D=Delirium assess, prevent, manage; E=Early mobility and Exercise; F=Family engagement. Each 10% increase in bundle compliance reduces 28-day mortality by 15%, delirium by 17%, and mechanical ventilation by 16% (Pun et al. 2019).
src: Pun et al. Critical Care Medicine 2019

def: Calculated cation-anion difference: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3). Normal: 8-12 mEq/L (12 mEq/L with albumin 4 g/dL). High AG metabolic acidosis (MUDPILES): Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates. Correct for hypoalbuminemia: add 2.5 mEq for every 1 g/dL albumin below 4. If high AG + metabolic alkalosis coexist, check delta-delta ratio.

Acute inflammatory lung injury defined by Berlin criteria: acute onset (<1 week), bilateral opacities on CXR/CT, respiratory failure not fully explained by heart failure/fluid overload, PaO2/FiO2 ratio: mild 200-300, moderate 100-200, severe <100 (all on PEEP ≥5). Cornerstone treatment: lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW, plateau <30 cmH2O). Mortality ~40% for severe ARDS. PROSEVA: prone positioning >16h/day reduces mortality in P/F<150.
src: ARDS Network ARMA Trial 2000 / Berlin Definition 2012

def: Respiratory-system driving pressure (ΔP) = Plateau pressure - PEEP (equals tidal volume / respiratory-system compliance).

Acute brain dysfunction characterized by disturbed attention, cognition, and awareness. Affects 50-80% of ICU patients. Assessed by CAM-ICU or ICDSC. Three subtypes: hyperactive (agitated), hypoactive (quiet — more common, worse prognosis), mixed. Non-pharmacologic prevention (ABCDEF bundle, lighting, reorientation) is preferred. If pharmacologic treatment needed: haloperidol (evidence limited) or quetiapine. Avoid physical restraints. Benzodiazepines worsen delirium.

Acute metabolic emergency: glucose >250 mg/dL, pH <7.3, bicarb <15 mEq/L, anion gap >12, ketonemia/ketonuria. Management: IV fluids (1-2L NS first hour), insulin drip 0.1 u/kg/h (do NOT use loading dose), add D5W when glucose <200 mg/dL, replace K+ if <3.5, supplement phosphate. Transition to SQ insulin when: anion gap closed, patient tolerating PO, overlap 1-2h. Most common precipitant: infection.

Overview: Hypokalemia (<3.5 mEq/L): ECG changes (U waves, flat T waves), replace 10-20 mEq/h IV, 40 mEq PO q4-6h; correct Mg first. Hypomagnesemia (<1.7 mg/dL): stabilizes K+ channels — must correct before K+ repletion. Hyponatremia (<135): correction max 6-8 mEq/L/day (risk ODS/central pontine myelinolysis). Hyperkalemia (>6 or with ECG changes): C-BIG-K protocol. These are all high-yield on BCPS.

Neurological assessment tool: Eyes (1-4: none/pain/voice/spontaneous) + Verbal (1-5: none/incomprehensible/inappropriate/confused/oriented) + Motor (1-6: none/extension/flexion/withdrawal/localizes/obeys). Total 3-15. GCS ≤8 = severe TBI, indicates intubation consideration. GCS 9-12 = moderate. GCS 13-15 = mild. Not valid in sedated/paralyzed patients (use RASS instead for ICU).

C-BIG-K protocol for K+ >6.5 or with ECG changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave): C=Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV (membrane stabilization, acts in minutes); B=Sodium Bicarbonate (shifts K+ intracellularly, useful if acidemic); I=Insulin 10 units + Glucose 25g IV (shifts K+ into cells, -0.5 mEq/L in 30-60 min); G=already covered; K=Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) or patiromer/sodium zirconium (bind K+ in gut); Dialysis for refractory.

Serum Na <135 mEq/L. First assess volume status. SIADH (euvolemic): fluid restriction ± salt tabs ± tolvaptan (V2-receptor antagonist); loop diuretics can help. Hypovolemic: IV NS or oral sodium. Hypervolemic (HF, cirrhosis): fluid restriction, loop diuretics. Correction rate: MAX 6-8 mEq/L per day (10-12 mEq/L per 24h per some guidelines). Rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/central pontine myelinolysis) especially in chronic hyponatremia.

Lactate >2 mmol/L (plus vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation) = septic shock criterion (Sepsis-3); lactate >4 mmol/L reflects severe hypoperfusion and markedly higher mortality but is not the Sepsis-3 threshold.

Cornerstone ARDS management from the ARDSNet ARMA trial (2000): Vt 6 mL/kg IBW (USE IDEAL BODY WEIGHT, not actual body weight); plateau pressure <30 cmH2O; PEEP ≥5 cmH2O (higher for severe ARDS per PEEP tables). Reduces VILI (ventilator-induced lung injury). Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable (pH 7.25-7.30 tolerated). Driving pressure target <15 cmH2O. High PEEP tables (ALVEOLI) did not improve mortality but improved oxygenation.

First-line vasopressor for septic shock (SOAP-II trial confirmed NE superior to dopamine). Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction, ↑SVR) > beta-1 (mild ↑HR, ↑contractility). Dose: 0.01-3 mcg/kg/min. Lacks beta-2 effects, minimal HR increase. If MAP target not achieved with NE alone: add vasopressin 0.03-0.04 units/min (first add-on, spares NE). If still inadequate: epinephrine or angiotensin II. Central line or large peripheral IV preferred.

PaO2/FiO2 ratio — key oxygenation index used in ARDS Berlin classification. Normal: ~500 mmHg. Mild ARDS: 200-300; Moderate: 100-200; Severe: <100. All measured on PEEP ≥5. Calculate: if PaO2 60 mmHg on FiO2 0.5 = P/F 120 (moderate ARDS). Limitations: not always available (need ABG). SpO2/FiO2 (S/F) ratio can substitute when ABG unavailable (S/F 235 ≈ P/F 200).

Pressure maintained in the airways at end of exhalation during mechanical ventilation. Prevents alveolar collapse (atelectotrauma), recruits collapsed alveoli, improves oxygenation. Minimum PEEP 5 cmH2O for ARDS. Higher PEEP (10-20) for moderate-severe ARDS (per ARDSNet PEEP/FiO2 table). Risk: overdistension and hemodynamic compromise (↓venous return, ↓CO). Optimal PEEP titrated by driving pressure minimization or compliance curves.

Placing mechanically ventilated ARDS patients face-down (prone) for ≥16 hours/day. PROSEVA trial (2013): 28-day mortality 16% vs 32.8% for supine in severe ARDS (P/F <150). Mechanism: redistributes ventilation to dorsal lung, recruits posterior segments, reduces V/Q mismatch. Requires dedicated nursing team and careful ETT management. Contraindications: spinal instability, open abdomen, facial trauma, hemodynamic instability.

Gold-standard ICU sedation assessment tool. Scores: +4 (combative), +3 (very agitated), +2 (agitated), +1 (restless), 0 (alert/calm), -1 (drowsy), -2 (light sedation), -3 (moderate sedation), -4 (deep sedation), -5 (unarousable). Target for most mechanically ventilated patients: RASS -1 to 0 (light sedation). Lighter sedation targets reduce ventilator days and ICU-acquired weakness. Document RASS before and after sedation medication changes.
histamine-2 blockers (famotidine) an alternative; the PEPTIC trial found no clear mortality difference between PPI and H2 blocker (SUP-ICU compared pantoprazole vs placebo)

Post-cardiac arrest neurological protection strategy. TTM2 trial (2021): active cooling to 33°C showed NO benefit over targeted normothermia (≤37.7°C) for 6-month mortality or neurological outcomes. Current recommendation: prevent fever >37.7°C for 72h post-ROSC; active cooling to 33°C is NOT routinely required. Continuous temperature monitoring essential. Antipyretics + surface cooling pads maintain normothermia.

Endogenous antidiuretic hormone; as vasopressor: V1 receptor mediated vasoconstriction. Second-line vasopressor added to norepinephrine in septic shock (VANISH trial: vasopressin allows earlier NE weaning; VASST: may benefit septic shock patients who are less severe). Dose: fixed 0.03-0.04 units/min (NOT titrated). Benefit: NE-sparing; may improve renal outcomes (V2 receptor). Avoid in cardiogenic shock (↑ afterload without ↑ CO). Terlipressin: used in hepatorenal syndrome.

Extreme hyperglycemia (typically >600 mg/dL) with hyperosmolarity (>320 mOsm/kg), minimal ketosis, and severe dehydration. Occurs in type 2 DM; often precipitated by infection, inadequate hydration, or medications (steroids, thiazides). Serum osmolality = 2×Na + BUN/2.8 + glucose/18. Treatment: aggressive fluid replacement (NS first, then 0.45% NS), cautious insulin after fluids started, electrolyte monitoring. Gradual glucose correction; avoid rapid osmolality changes.

Life-threatening thyroid hormone excess (Burch-Wartofsky score ≥45 = likely thyroid storm). Precipitants: surgery, infection, radioactive iodine, contrast. Treatment sequence (SSKI must come AFTER PTU/methimazole to block hormone release): 1. PTU 200mg q4-6h (also blocks T4→T3 conversion, preferred over methimazole acutely); 2. After PTU: SSKI 5 drops q6h or Lugol's solution; 3. Dexamethasone 2mg q6h (blocks conversion); 4. Beta-blocker (propranolol preferred — also blocks conversion); 5. ICU monitoring.

Deficient thyroid hormone production. Primary: elevated TSH + low free T4 (thyroid gland failure, most common — Hashimoto's). Secondary: low TSH + low free T4 (pituitary failure). Treatment: levothyroxine (T4) 1.6 mcg/kg/day. TSH target: 0.5-2.5 mIU/L (young); 1-4 mIU/L (elderly). Myxedema coma: IV levothyroxine 200-400 mcg loading + IV T3 (liothyronine) + IV hydrocortisone 100mg q8h + ICU support + identify/treat precipitant.

Excess thyroid hormone. Most common: Graves disease (autoimmune TSH-receptor antibody stimulation), toxic multinodular goiter, thyroiditis. Low TSH + elevated free T4/T3. Treatment options: antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred; PTU in 1st trimester due to methimazole embryopathy); radioactive iodine (RAI — contraindicated in pregnancy); surgery. Beta-blocker for symptomatic control (propranolol or atenolol). Methimazole: agranulocytosis (CBC if fever/sore throat).

Most common cause of hyperthyroidism (~80%). Autoimmune production of thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) that activate TSH receptors. Clinical features: goiter, exophthalmos (proptosis), pretibial myxedema. Labs: suppressed TSH, elevated free T4/T3, positive TSI or TSHR antibodies. Treatment: methimazole (preferred over PTU in non-pregnant adults), RAI, or thyroidectomy. Remission rate on antithyroid drugs: 30-40% after 12-18 months.

Basal-bolus insulin is gold standard for inpatient hyperglycemia. Basal insulins: glargine (U-100/U-300), detemir, degludec (longest t½ ~42h). Bolus insulins: aspart, lispro, glulisine (rapid-acting, use with meals). Avoid NPH as basal (unpredictable peaks). Insulin correction scale acceptable for supplemental but NOT as primary treatment. Inpatient glucose targets: 140-180 mg/dL (non-ICU); 140-180 mg/dL (ICU — avoids hypoglycemia of tighter control per NICE-SUGAR). Hypoglycemia: D50 25-50 mL IV or glucagon 1mg IM/SQ.

Synthetic T4 (thyroxine) for hypothyroidism replacement. Standard dose: 1.6 mcg/kg/day. Absorption: take on empty stomach 30-60 min before breakfast (or ≥4h after calcium, iron, cholestyramine, antacids — all bind and reduce absorption). T4 → T3 peripherally in tissues. Many drugs affect requirements: rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin (accelerate clearance); amiodarone (blocks conversion). Monitor TSH 4-6 weeks after any dose change. Pregnancy increases requirements by 25-30%.

Thionamide antithyroid drug that blocks thyroid peroxidase enzyme, inhibiting thyroid hormone synthesis. Drug of choice for hyperthyroidism in non-pregnant adults. Dose: 10-30mg daily. Onset of action: 2-4 weeks (reduces new hormone synthesis; existing hormone stores continue releasing). Does NOT block T4→T3 conversion (PTU does). Key ADRs: agranulocytosis (<1%), rash, hepatotoxicity. Avoid in 1st trimester (embryopathy — aplasia cutis) — use PTU instead.

Dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, saxagliptin, alogliptin, linagliptin) block degradation of endogenous GLP-1 and GIP, increasing insulin release in a glucose-dependent manner (low hypoglycemia risk). Modest HbA1c reduction (~0.5-0.8%). Weight neutral. CV safety established (EXAMINE, SAVOR-TIMI, TECOS): neutral CV outcomes. Saxagliptin: small ↑ HF hospitalization (SAVOR-TIMI). Linagliptin: only agent NOT renally eliminated — safe in CKD without dose adjustment.

GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide, tirzepatide) stimulate insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying, reduce appetite. HbA1c reduction: 1-2%. Weight loss: 3-6 kg (liraglutide), 6-7 kg (semaglutide), 9-11 kg (tirzepatide). CV benefit: LEADER (liraglutide), SUSTAIN-6 (sema), REWIND (dulaglutide) — anti-atherosclerotic, not hemodynamic. First-line if ASCVD. ADRs: nausea (start low/go slow), pancreatitis (rare), injection site reactions. Semaglutide (Ozempic) weekly; liraglutide (Victoza) daily.

Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) block renal glucose reabsorption (glucosuria). HbA1c reduction: 0.7-1%. Weight loss: 2-3 kg. CV outcomes: EMPA-REG (empagliflozin → 38% ↓ CV death), CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD (CKD protection even without DM). Key class effects: HF hospitalization reduction, CKD protection. ADRs: genital mycotic infections, UTI, DKA (euglycemic — hold before surgery), Fournier's gangrene. Avoid if eGFR <20 (initiation).
src: ADA Standards 2024 / Multiple CV Outcome Trials

def: Insulin secretagogues (glipizide, glyburide, glimepiride) that close K+ATP channels on pancreatic beta cells → depolarization → calcium influx → insulin release (glucose-independent). HbA1c reduction: 1-1.5%. Major ADR: hypoglycemia (significant, especially glyburide — long-acting; avoid in elderly/CKD). Weight gain ~2 kg. No CV benefit demonstrated. Avoid glyburide in CKD (active metabolite accumulates). Glipizide preferred in elderly (shorter t½, no active metabolites).

Synthetic glucocorticoids (prednisone, methylprednisolone, dexamethasone, hydrocortisone) with anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant effects. Potency comparison (anti-inflammatory): cortisol 1, prednisone 4, methylprednisolone 5, dexamethasone 25. Equivalent doses: cortisol 20mg = prednisone 5mg = methylprednisolone 4mg = dexamethasone 0.75mg. ADRs: hyperglycemia, HPA suppression (taper if >2-3 weeks), osteoporosis, hypertension, infection risk, cataracts, psychiatric effects.

Deficiency of adrenal cortical hormones. Primary (Addison's disease): cortisol + aldosterone deficiency (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hyperpigmentation). Secondary: cortisol deficiency only (from pituitary failure or steroid suppression). Stress dosing during illness/surgery: hydrocortisone 50-100mg q8h (stress dose) then taper. Chronic replacement: hydrocortisone 15-25mg/day divided doses + fludrocortisone 0.1mg/day (primary only). Adrenal crisis: hydrocortisone 100mg IV bolus + IVF + electrolyte correction.

Neurological disorder with recurrent unprovoked seizures. Classification: focal (with/without impaired awareness) vs generalized (absence, myoclonic, tonic-clonic). First-line agents: levetiracetam (LEV — broad spectrum, minimal interactions, safe in pregnancy), lamotrigine (preferred women of childbearing age), valproate (most effective for generalized but teratogenic). Enzyme inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) reduce levels of many drugs including oral contraceptives.
Classic narrow therapeutic index drug (target total level 10-20 mcg/mL; free/unbound target 1-2 mcg/mL; correct total for low albumin).

Seizure lasting >5 minutes OR two seizures without return to baseline. Medical emergency. Treatment ladder: 1st line (0-5 min): benzodiazepines — lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4mg) or midazolam 10mg IM (RAMPART trial — IM midazolam non-inferior to IV lorazepam, faster in prehospital). 2nd line (5-20 min): fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg IV OR levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV OR valproate 40 mg/kg IV (ESETT trial: all three equally effective). 3rd line (>30 min): phenobarbital, propofol, midazolam infusion, ketamine.

Cerebrovascular event from thromboembolism or atherosclerosis. NIHSS quantifies severity. Thrombolysis (IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg, max 90mg): <4.5h from onset (or last known well); contraindications include BP >185/110 (must treat first), recent surgery, active bleeding, anticoagulation with therapeutic INR. Thrombectomy (mechanical): for large vessel occlusion (LVO) up to 24h with imaging selection (DAWN/DEFUSE-3). Antiplatelets: aspirin 325mg within 24-48h of onset (IST, CAST trials).

11-item neurological examination tool (0-42) assessing stroke severity: consciousness, gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, arm/leg motor, limb ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, extinction/neglect. Score 0-4: minor; 5-15: moderate; 16-20: moderate-severe; >20: severe. Calculated before administering thrombolysis. Not validated in isolated posterior circulation strokes. Can be completed in <7 minutes by certified provider.
src: AHA Stroke Guidelines / Brott et al. Stroke 1989

def: Functional outcome scale for stroke patients: 0=no symptoms; 1=no significant disability; 2=slight disability (independent); 3=moderate disability (requires some help); 4=moderately severe (unable to walk independently); 5=severe disability (bedridden, incontinent); 6=dead. Primary outcome in DAWN/DEFUSE-3 thrombectomy trials. Favorable outcome: mRS 0-2. Important to assess at 90 days for clinical trials.

Transient neurological deficit from focal brain ischemia without infarction, resolving completely (most within 24h, often minutes). Risk of stroke highest in first 48h (ABCD2 ≥4 = high risk). Urgent evaluation required: ABCD2 score, MRI/DWI (detects small infarcts), carotid imaging, cardiac monitoring for AF. DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21-90 days after high-risk TIA/minor stroke then aspirin alone (POINT/CHANCE trials). Anticoagulate if AF found.

Broad-spectrum antiseizure medication (SV2A ligand) with favorable pharmacokinetic profile: no hepatic metabolism, ~66% renal elimination, no significant drug interactions, linear kinetics. Effective for partial, generalized tonic-clonic, and myoclonic seizures. ADRs: behavioral (irritability, aggression — especially in patients with prior psychiatric history; managed with dose reduction or pyridoxine). Renally dose-adjust (reduce by 50% if CrCl 30-50; by 75% if CrCl <30).

Broad-spectrum antiepileptic (multiple mechanisms: Na+ channel, GABA enhancement, T-type Ca2+ channels). Most effective agent for generalized epilepsy including juvenile myoclonic epilepsy. Narrow TI: target trough 50-100 mcg/mL. TERATOGENIC (category D/X): neural tube defects 1-2%, cognitive impairment — avoid in women of childbearing potential if possible; require folate 4mg/day. ADRs: hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia, weight gain, hair loss, tremor. Strong inhibitor of CYP2C9.

Sodium channel blocker with additional inhibition of voltage-dependent calcium channels. Broad-spectrum: effective for focal, generalized, and absence seizures. Preferred first-line for women of childbearing age (safer pregnancy profile than valproate). Slow titration required (4-8 weeks) to reduce risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN (especially if combined with valproate — VPA dramatically increases lamotrigine levels by inhibiting glucuronidation → reduce lamotrigine dose by 50%). Enzyme inducers (phenytoin, carbamazepine) decrease lamotrigine levels.

Pain caused by damage or disease affecting the somatosensory nervous system (diabetic neuropathy, PHN, fibromyalgia). First-line: gabapentin/pregabalin (alpha-2-delta calcium channel subunit ligands; reduce neuronal excitability); duloxetine (SNRI; best evidence for DPN and fibromyalgia); tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline — anticholinergic, avoid in elderly). Opioids are third-line (tolerance, dependence). Topical lidocaine or capsaicin for localized PHN. SNRIs preferred over gabapentinoids for fibromyalgia.

Acute decline in kidney function: KDIGO criteria — increase in SCr by ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6h. Stages: 1 (1.5-1.9× baseline), 2 (2.0-2.9×), 3 (≥3× or SCr ≥4 or dialysis). Common causes: prerenal (most common: volume depletion, hypotension — 60%), intrinsic (ATN from ischemia/nephrotoxins, GN), postrenal (obstruction). Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast, vancomycin + pip-tazo).

Kidney damage or GFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² for >3 months. Staged by eGFR (G1-G5) and albuminuria (A1-A3). Stage G3a: eGFR 45-59; G3b: 30-44; G4: 15-29; G5: <15. Drug dosing adjustments essential: use Cockcroft-Gault (not MDRD) for drug dosing. RAAS inhibition with ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i slows progression. Complications: anemia (EPO deficiency → ESA + iron), hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, secondary hyperparathyroidism, CKD-MBD. KDIGO 2024: start SGLT2i if eGFR ≥20 + UACR ≥200.

Estimated kidney filtration rate calculated from serum creatinine using CKD-EPI (2021 equation — removed race variable) or MDRD formula. Units: mL/min/1.73m². Reported by labs and used for CKD staging and cardiology drug initiation thresholds. IMPORTANT: For drug dosing in patients, use Cockcroft-Gault (CrCl) rather than eGFR — different equation, includes weight, important for renally-eliminated drugs. eGFR and CrCl can give significantly different values in extremes of weight/age.

Removal of drugs during intermittent hemodialysis depends on: molecular weight (smaller = more cleared), protein binding (free drug cleared), Vd (large Vd = less cleared — amiodarone), water solubility. HD-cleared drugs requiring supplemental doses post-HD: vancomycin (re-dose after HD session if trough <15), aminoglycosides (dose after HD), levetiracetam (supplemental dose 250-500mg), lithium (dose after). Vancomycin clearance depends on membrane type: high-flux dialyzers remove vancomycin (re-dose after HD if trough low), whereas older low-flux membranes remove little. Always check PK reference for dialysis dosing.

K+ >5.5 mEq/L. Common in CKD, DM, RAAS inhibition, trimethoprim, K+-sparing diuretics. ECG changes: peaked T waves → PR prolongation → wide QRS → sine wave → VF (progressive danger). Newer agents: patiromer (Veltassa) and sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) — safer than sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate — risk of intestinal necrosis, especially post-op). These allow chronic K+-lowering enabling RAAS/MRA therapy in CKD/HF patients with hyperkalemia.

Furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide, ethacrynic acid — inhibit Na+K+2Cl- cotransporter in thick ascending limb (NKCC2). Most potent diuretics. Torsemide: oral bioavailability ~80% (vs furosemide 10-80%, variable). Use torsemide or bumetanide when poor oral furosemide response. Dose equivalence: furosemide 40mg = bumetanide 1mg = torsemide 20mg. Ototoxicity (especially with aminoglycosides — synergistic). Continuous infusion ± thiazide for diuretic resistance in HF (metolazone synergy: give 30 min before loop diuretic).

Euvolemic hyponatremia from non-physiological ADH secretion. Causes: pulmonary disease (pneumonia, SCLC — ectopic ADH), CNS disorders, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, chlorpropamide, vincristine). Diagnosis: serum osm <275, urine osm >100, urine Na >40 mEq/L, euvolemia, normal adrenal/thyroid function. Treatment: fluid restriction (1-1.5L/day) first-line; salt tabs 1-3g/day; loop diuretics; vaptan (tolvaptan/conivaptan) for refractory; demeclocycline (rarely used). Na correction max 6-8 mEq/L/day.

Vancomycin causes nephrotoxicity via tubular accumulation and oxidative stress, particularly at AUC24 >650 mg·h/L or trough >20 mcg/mL. Risk markedly amplified when combined with piperacillin-tazobactam (combination increases AKI risk 4-fold vs either agent alone — use with caution in high-risk patients). Monitor SCr every 24-48h in ICU, every 48-72h in non-ICU. If SCr rises ≥0.5 mg/dL: hold next dose, reassess. High-risk: critical illness, obesity, prolonged therapy >7 days.

Immune-mediated platelet destruction and paradoxical thrombosis caused by IgG antibodies against heparin-platelet factor 4 (PF4) complexes. 4T score (Thrombocytopenia, Timing [5-10 days], Thrombosis, oTher cause) stratifies pre-test probability. STOP all heparin (including flushes, LMWH). Use alternative anticoagulant: argatroban (hepatic elimination, preferred in renal failure) or bivalirudin. Fondaparinux (limited data). Warfarin is CONTRAINDICATED acutely (paradoxical thrombosis — start only after platelets >150). Wait ≥3 months before re-exposing to heparin.

Reversal strategies for warfarin over-anticoagulation. Life-threatening bleeding (INR any level): 4-factor PCC (Kcentra) 25-50 units/kg IV PLUS vitamin K1 10mg IV slow infusion (1mg/min). PCC is faster than FFP and avoids volume overload. Non-life-threatening bleeding with elevated INR: vitamin K 2.5-5mg PO (INR 4.5-10) or 5-10mg PO/IV. Asymptomatic elevated INR 4-10: hold 1-2 doses ± vitamin K 1-2.5mg PO. Goal: INR ≤1.4 for urgent procedures; ≤2 for neuraxial anesthesia.

Concentrated factors II, VII, IX, X plus protein C and S. Preferred for rapid reversal of warfarin in life-threatening bleeding (faster INR reversal than FFP: INR corrected in minutes vs hours). Dose: 25 units/kg for INR 2-4; 35 units/kg for INR 4-6; 50 units/kg for INR >6 (max 5000 units). Also used off-label for DOAC reversal in emergent settings where specific reversal agents not available. Risks: thrombotic events (boxed warning), heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (contains heparin).
Lab TLS: ≥2 of: uric acid ≥8 mg/dL, K ≥6 mEq/L, phosphorus ≥4.5 mg/dL (adults; ≥6.5 mg/dL in children), calcium ≤7 mg/dL.

See Neutropenic Fever — single temp ≥38.3°C or ≥38°C × 1h with ANC <500 or expected nadir <500. Empiric antipseudomonal therapy within 60 min. MASCC score ≥21 = low-risk (may manage outpatient with oral ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate or fluoroquinolone alone). High-risk (MASCC <21, anticipated nadir <100 × >7 days, solid tumor/lymphoma patients): admit for IV antibiotics. G-CSF: start 24-72h AFTER chemotherapy completion, not with active febrile neutropenia.

Chronic inflammatory airway disease with variable expiratory airflow limitation. GINA 2024: assess control and risk, preferred reliever is low-dose ICS-formoterol (not SABA alone — SABA monotherapy increases exacerbation risk, per SMART trial). Step 1-2: PRN low-dose ICS-formoterol. Step 3: daily low-dose ICS-LABA. Step 4: medium ICS-LABA. Step 5: high ICS-LABA ± biologics (anti-IL5 for eosinophilic: mepolizumab, benralizumab; anti-IgE: omalizumab). Exacerbation: SABA + systemic steroids 5-7 days.

Progressive obstructive airflow limitation from chronic airway/parenchymal inflammation (emphysema + chronic bronchitis). GOLD classification: A-E by symptoms and exacerbation history. FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator. Initial: LAMA (tiotropium) preferred over LABA for dyspnea. Group E (frequent exacerbations): LAMA+LABA → add ICS if eosinophils ≥300. Triple therapy (ICS+LAMA+LABA) reduces exacerbations and mortality (ETHOS, IMPACT trials). Roflumilast (PDE-4 inhibitor): for frequent exacerbators with FEV1 <50% + chronic bronchitis.

First-line anti-inflammatory treatment for persistent asthma (fluticasone, budesonide, beclomethasone, mometasone). Reduce airway inflammation, exacerbation frequency, and oral steroid use. NOT bronchodilators — do not use PRN for rescue (except ICS-formoterol in GINA 2024 approach). ADRs: oropharyngeal candidiasis (rinse mouth after use), dysphonia. High-dose ICS: systemic effects (adrenal suppression, cataracts, bone density). In COPD: add ICS only if eosinophils ≥300 or frequent exacerbations on dual bronchodilators.

Beta-2 agonists with prolonged duration (12-24h): salmeterol, formoterol (12h), indacaterol, vilanterol (24h). Bronchodilators that relax airway smooth muscle. In asthma: ONLY use combined with ICS (never monotherapy — SMART trial: LABA monotherapy increases asthma-related deaths; Black Box Warning). In COPD: can use as monotherapy or with LAMA. Formoterol is fast-onset LABA (can be used for rescue in ICS-formoterol combinations per GINA 2024).

Anticholinergic bronchodilators that block M3 muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle (tiotropium, umeclidinium, glycopyrronium, aclidinium). Primary treatment for COPD; superior to LABA for dyspnea reduction and exacerbation prevention. Not first-line for asthma (tiotropium has add-on benefit at Step 4-5). ADRs: dry mouth (most common), urinary retention (caution in BPH), constipation, tachycardia. Avoid with narrow-angle glaucoma. Tiotropium: HandiHaler or Respimat (once-daily). UPLIFT trial: tiotropium reduced exacerbations in COPD.

Arterial blood gas analysis steps: 1. pH (7.35-7.45); 2. pCO2 (35-45 — respiratory); 3. HCO3 (22-26 — metabolic); 4. Check compensation (Winter's formula for metabolic acidosis: expected pCO2 = 1.5×HCO3 + 8 ±2). Metabolic acidosis: ↓pH, ↓HCO3, ↓pCO2 (compensatory). Respiratory acidosis: ↓pH, ↑pCO2, ↑HCO3 (compensatory). Also assess oxygenation: PaO2 (normal 80-100), A-a gradient. Mixed disorders common in critically ill.

Natriuretic peptides released from ventricular myocardium in response to wall stress/volume overload. BNP <100 pg/mL: HF unlikely; >400: HF likely. NT-proBNP: <300 rules out HF; ≥450 (age <50), ≥900 (50-75), ≥1800 (>75) supports HF. Both used to diagnose and monitor HF. Levels affected by: obesity (lower levels), renal failure (higher), AF (higher). NT-proBNP has longer half-life and better stability. Values used to guide therapy changes and prognostication in HF.

Cardiac-specific proteins released during myocardial injury. High-sensitivity troponin (hsTn) detectable within 1-2h, peaks 12-24h, remains elevated 7-14 days. Serial troponins (0/1h or 0/2h protocol) rule in/out NSTEMI. Elevated in: ACS, myocarditis, PE, sepsis, CKD (reduced clearance). Fourth Universal Definition of MI: rise/fall of Tn with at least one value above 99th percentile URL. Type 1 MI: plaque rupture/thrombosis; Type 2 MI: supply/demand mismatch (tachycardia, anemia, sepsis).
Standardized measure of the extrinsic coagulation pathway (factors II, VII, X, and fibrinogen); factors II, VII, IX, and X are the vitamin K-dependent factors.

PCT <0.25 mcg/L: bacterial infection unlikely; PCT 0.25-0.5: bacterial infection unlikely but possible; PCT 0.5-2: localized infection possible; PCT >2: strong evidence for systemic bacterial infection/sepsis; PCT >10: high likelihood of severe sepsis/septic shock. Rising PCT suggests ongoing bacterial infection; falling PCT confirms adequate treatment response. Useful for guiding antibiotic discontinuation — reduces duration by 2-3 days without worse outcomes.

Waste product from muscle creatine metabolism filtered freely by glomeruli and not reabsorbed. Serum creatinine (0.6-1.2 mg/dL in adults) is inversely related to GFR — it doubles when GFR halves. Affected by: muscle mass (low in elderly, women, amputees → lower SCr despite significant CKD), diet (creatine supplementation), certain medications (trimethoprim blocks tubular creatinine secretion → ↑SCr without true GFR change). Always interpret in context of baseline, trends, and clinical picture.

Normal venous lactate: 0.5-1.5 mmol/L (some labs use 0.5-2.0). Elevated (>2 mmol/L) indicates anaerobic metabolism (tissue hypoperfusion), impaired clearance (liver failure), or increased production (seizures, intense exercise, certain drugs — metformin in high doses/renal failure, linezolid with mitochondrial toxicity). Serial measurement guides resuscitation in sepsis — target clearance ≥10% per 2 hours. Lactate alone does not diagnose septic shock; clinical context essential.

Panel assessing hepatocellular injury (ALT, AST — hepatocyte damage), cholestasis (ALP, GGT, direct bilirubin), and synthetic function (albumin, PT/INR — most important for liver synthesis). AST/ALT >2:1 (alcoholic hepatitis). Isolated ALP elevation: think biliary obstruction or bone disease. Drug-induced liver injury (DILI): R-ratio = (ALT/ULN) / (ALP/ULN) — >5: hepatocellular; <2: cholestatic; 2-5: mixed. Critically, aminotransferases can be normal in cirrhosis (all hepatocytes already damaged).

Acute Coronary Syndrome — umbrella term for STEMI (ST-elevation myocardial infarction), NSTEMI (non-ST-elevation MI), and unstable angina. Caused by atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis. Management involves antiplatelets (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation, and revascularization (PCI for STEMI within 90 min door-to-balloon). See individual entries for STEMI/NSTEMI management.

Dual Antiplatelet Therapy — aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, prasugrel, or ticagrelor). Standard post-ACS or post-PCI duration: 12 months (then aspirin indefinitely). Can shorten to 6 months if high bleeding risk. Can extend >12 months if high ischemic/low bleeding risk (PEGASUS: ticagrelor 60mg BID up to 3 years). After stent: DAPT prevents in-stent thrombosis.

Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy — evidence-based drug combinations proven to reduce mortality and hospitalizations in HFrEF (EF <40%). Four pillars: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, or bisoprolol), MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone), and SGLT2i (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin). Simultaneous initiation at low doses and rapid uptitration is preferred over sequential addition (STRONG-HF concept).

Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System — hormonal cascade regulating blood pressure and fluid balance. Renin (released by juxtaglomerular cells with ↓renal perfusion/↓Na+) cleaves angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I → ACE → Angiotensin II (vasoconstriction, aldosterone release). Blocked by: ACEi (block ACE), ARB (block AT1 receptor), MRA (block aldosterone receptor), direct renin inhibitors (aliskiren). AVOID: ACEi + ARB (dual RAAS blockade — ONTARGET, ALTITUDE — harm).

Therapeutic Drug Monitoring — systematic measurement of drug concentrations to individualize dosing and ensure therapeutic efficacy while avoiding toxicity. Applied to drugs with narrow therapeutic indices, significant PK variability, or difficult-to-assess clinical endpoints. Key BCPS drugs: vancomycin (AUC/MIC), aminoglycosides (peak/trough), digoxin (0.5-0.8 ng/mL), phenytoin (10-20 mcg/mL free-corrected), cyclosporine/tacrolimus (troughs), lithium (0.6-1.2 mEq/L).

Medication Therapy Management — pharmacist-provided patient-centered services to optimize therapeutic outcomes. Components: Comprehensive Medication Review (CMR), Targeted Medication Review (TMR), Personal Medication Record (PMR), Medication Action Plan (MAP), documentation and follow-up. Five medication therapy problems: unnecessary drug therapy, needs additional therapy, ineffective drug, dosage too low, dosage too high (ADR/toxicity), noncompliance. Medicare Part D MTM eligibility: multiple chronic conditions, multiple drugs, likely to incur high drug costs.

Pharmacists Patient Care Process — standard framework for pharmacist patient care: 1. Collect (patient history, medications, labs, preferences); 2. Assess (drug therapy problems, clinical status, goals); 3. Plan (evidence-based therapy recommendations); 4. Implement (education, counseling, prescribing if authorized); 5. Follow-up: Monitor and Evaluate. Aligns pharmacy care with other health professions' care models. Foundational for collaborative practice agreements and MTM.

Quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment — bedside screening tool for sepsis outside ICU: Altered mentation (GCS <15), Respiratory rate ≥22/min, Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg. Score ≥2 predicts increased mortality and organ dysfunction. NOT the diagnostic criterion for sepsis (SOFA increase ≥2 is); rather a prompt to assess for sepsis and initiate workup. Simpler than full SOFA, calculable at bedside without labs. Sensitivity ~50-70%; use with clinical judgment.

Sequential Organ Failure Assessment — 6-organ scoring system (respiration, coagulation, liver, cardiovascular, CNS, renal). Each organ scored 0-4; total 0-24. Baseline SOFA ≥2 defines organ dysfunction; increase ≥2 from baseline defines sepsis. Daily SOFA tracks trajectory — improving SOFA correlates with survival. Components: PaO2/FiO2 (respiratory), platelets (coagulation), bilirubin (liver), MAP/vasopressors (cardiovascular), GCS (neuro), creatinine/UO (renal).

New York Heart Association functional classification for heart failure severity: Class I — no symptoms with ordinary activity; Class II — slight limitation of physical activity, comfortable at rest; Class III — marked limitation, less than ordinary activity causes symptoms; Class IV — symptoms at rest or with any physical activity. Used to guide HF therapy intensity (e.g., RALES: spironolactone in NYHA III-IV; EMPHASIS-HF: eplerenone in NYHA II) and device therapy decisions.

Venous Thromboembolism — includes deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE). Risk stratification: Virchow's triad (stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury). Provoked VTE: treat 3 months. Unprovoked: 3-6 months minimum, consider indefinite. Cancer-associated: prefer DOAC (rivaroxaban, edoxaban) or LMWH. Prophylaxis: pharmacological (LMWH or UFH) + mechanical (graduated compression stockings, IPC devices). DOAC for treatment: apixaban or rivaroxaban (no parenteral lead-in needed).

Individualized pharmacokinetic modeling using patient-specific data (dose, timing, measured drug levels) combined with population PK parameters to estimate individual CL and Vd. Software (e.g., DoseMeRx, InsightRx, Precise PK) calculates the most likely PK parameters for an individual patient and projects AUC. ASHP/IDSA/SIDP 2020 guidelines recommend Bayesian software as the preferred method for vancomycin dosing and monitoring in serious MRSA infections. Reduces time-to-target and nephrotoxicity compared to trough-only monitoring.

QTc prolongation (>450ms men, >470ms women) predisposes to torsades de pointes (TdP) and sudden cardiac death. High-risk drugs: amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide, quinidine (Class IA/III antiarrhythmics); antipsychotics (haloperidol, ziprasidone, quetiapine); antibiotics (azithromycin, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole); methadone; ondansetron (>32mg IV — now avoided). Risk increases with hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, bradycardia, female sex, structural heart disease. Check CredibleMeds.org for risk categories.

Hepatotoxicity caused by medications, supplements, or xenobiotics. Most common cause of acute liver failure in developed countries. R-ratio determines pattern: R = (ALT/ULN)/(ALP/ULN); >5=hepatocellular, <2=cholestatic, 2-5=mixed. High-risk drugs: acetaminophen (dose-dependent, most common), isoniazid, methotrexate, amiodarone (phospholipidosis), rifampin, valproate, nitrofurantoin, statins (rare, usually benign). NAC is antidote for acetaminophen; no specific antidote for others.

Drugs with a small difference between the minimum effective concentration and the minimum toxic concentration, requiring careful monitoring. Key BCPS NTI drugs: digoxin (0.5-0.8 ng/mL), vancomycin (AUC 400-600), phenytoin (10-20 mcg/mL free), lithium (0.6-1.2 mEq/L acute, 0.4-1 mEq/L maintenance), warfarin (INR 2-3), cyclosporine/tacrolimus (variable), aminoglycosides (peak 8-10, trough <1). Generic substitution of NTI drugs requires caution — small changes in bioavailability can cause toxicity.

Potentially life-threatening drug interaction from excess serotonergic activity. Hunter Criteria (most sensitive/specific): serotonergic agent + one of: clonus (spontaneous, inducible, or ocular), agitation with hyperreflexia + diaphoresis, tremor + hyperreflexia, or hypertonia + fever (>38°C) + ocular/inducible clonus. Drug combinations: SSRIs/SNRIs + MAOIs (contraindicated); linezolid + SSRIs (linezolid = reversible MAO inhibitor); tramadol + SSRIs; triptans + SSRIs (mild risk); fentanyl + SNRIs. Treatment: stop offending drug, cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist), benzodiazepines, supportive care.

Severe mucocutaneous drug reaction with detachment of epidermis (<10% body surface area = SJS; 10-30% = SJS-TEN overlap; >30% = TEN). Mortality: SJS ~5%, TEN ~30%. High-risk drugs: aromatic antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine — slow titration critical), allopurinol (screen HLA-B*58:01 in Asian patients), sulfonamides (TMP-SMX), NSAIDs. Onset: 1-8 weeks after starting drug. Treatment: immediate drug withdrawal, transfer to burn unit, wound care, IV immunoglobulin or cyclosporine (controversial).
src: Roujeau et al. / FDA Safety Communications

def: Cumulative anticholinergic effects of multiple medications, particularly problematic in elderly patients (Beers Criteria). Anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, confusion, delirium, tachycardia. High-burden drugs: diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine, oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, imipramine), first-generation antipsychotics, cyclobenzaprine, benztropine, promethazine. Anticholinergic Burden Scale (ACB score) or Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden (ACB) scale quantifies risk. Prefer alternatives: loratadine over diphenhydramine, mirabegron over oxybutynin.
Strong inhibitors: azole antifungals (itraconazole, ketoconazole, posaconazole, voriconazole), clarithromycin, ritonavir, grapefruit juice (fluconazole is a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor).

Key CYP enzyme metabolizing: warfarin (S-enantiomer — more potent anticoagulant), phenytoin, NSAIDs (ibuprofen, celecoxib), losartan, glipizide, fluvastatin. Strong inhibitors: fluconazole (major — significantly increases warfarin effect), amiodarone, metronidazole, miconazole. Strong inducers: rifampin, carbamazepine. Pharmacogenomics: CYP2C9 *2/*3 poor metabolizers require lower warfarin doses; *2/*3 polymorphisms combined with VKORC1 influence warfarin dosing algorithms.

Polymorphic enzyme (poor metabolizers 7-10% Caucasians, ultrarapid metabolizers 1-7%).
