Metabolizes: codeine → morphine (ultrarapid metabolizers at risk for opioid toxicity; PM: no analgesia); tramadol; antidepressants (fluoxetine, paroxetine, venlafaxine); antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone); beta-blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol). Inhibitors (also act as substrates): paroxetine, fluoxetine, bupropion, cinacalcet. CYP2D6 cannot be induced. Tamoxifen: CYP2D6 converts to active metabolite endoxifen; PMs have lower efficacy.

Polymorphic enzyme metabolizing: clopidogrel (prodrug → active metabolite via CYP2C19 — loss-of-function alleles *2, *3 reduce antiplatelet effect; FDA Black Box Warning: test if possible), PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole — PMs have higher PPI levels = better acid suppression but not different clinical outcomes), voriconazole (nonlinear kinetics in PMs — dose adjust), escitalopram. Poor metabolizers: reduced clopidogrel efficacy → increased MACE risk post-ACS/PCI. Ultrarapid metabolizers: may need higher clopidogrel doses.

Dose-dependent hepatotoxicity from NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine) accumulation when glutathione stores depleted. Toxic ingestion: >150 mg/kg or >7.5g single dose. Rumack-Matthew nomogram plots serum level (4h post-ingestion minimum) vs time — above treatment line requires NAC. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) replenishes glutathione: IV protocol — 150 mg/kg over 1h → 12.5 mg/kg/h over 4h → 6.25 mg/kg/h over 16h. Oral: 140 mg/kg loading then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses. Start within 8-10h for maximum efficacy.

Classic triad: miosis (pinpoint pupils), respiratory depression, decreased consciousness. Naloxone is competitive opioid antagonist: 0.4-2 mg IM/IV/IN, repeat every 2-3 min. Duration of naloxone (30-90 min) shorter than most opioids — may need repeat dosing or infusion (two-thirds of reversal dose per hour). Extended-release/methadone/fentanyl: longer-acting, need prolonged monitoring. Buprenorphine high mu-receptor affinity — may need 2-10 mg naloxone. Naloxone cannot precipitate withdrawal in non-dependent individuals.

Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes: sodium channel blockade (QRS >100ms — risk of VT/VF), muscarinic blockade (anticholinergic), alpha-1 blockade (hypotension), histamine blockade (sedation). Treatment: sodium bicarbonate is cornerstone — bolus 1-2 mEq/kg IV to narrow QRS and treat arrhythmia (alkalinizes blood, reduces TCA binding to Na channels). Target serum pH 7.45-7.55. Avoid physostigmine (can precipitate seizures). Benzodiazepines for seizures. Lipid emulsion for refractory cardiovascular toxicity.

Both cause high anion gap metabolic acidosis via toxic metabolites (formaldehyde/formic acid from methanol; oxalic acid/glycolic acid from ethylene glycol — calcium oxalate crystals in urine → AKI). Elevated osmolal gap (early, before metabolism). Treatment: fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole — preferred ADH inhibitor; 15 mg/kg loading) or ethanol; folate for methanol (enhances formic acid metabolism); hemodialysis for severe cases (indications: methanol level >25 mg/dL, pH <7.15, renal failure, visual symptoms). Ethylene glycol: calcium supplementation (binds oxalate); pyridoxine + thiamine.

Rare but life-threatening complication of high-dose propofol infusions (>4-5 mg/kg/h for >48h). Characterized by: metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure (new right bundle branch block or ST changes), hypertriglyceridemia, renal failure, green urine/lipemia. Mechanism: mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired fatty acid oxidation. Prevention: limit to ≤4 mg/kg/h, supplement with alternative sedatives at high doses, monitor TG and CK every 48h in high-dose cases. Treatment: stop propofol immediately, supportive care, CRRT for severe cases.

Summary: Norepinephrine (alpha-1>>beta-1): first-line septic shock. Vasopressin (V1): add-on, NE-sparing, 0.03-0.04 units/min fixed dose. Epinephrine (alpha=beta-1): second-line or cardiogenic shock adjunct; increases lactate (muscle glycogenolysis, not tissue ischemia). Phenylephrine (pure alpha-1): SVT-associated hypotension, no tachycardia; avoid in sepsis/cardiogenic. Dobutamine (beta-1>>beta-2): inotrope for low CO/cardiogenic shock; reduces SVR. Angiotensin II: refractory vasodilatory shock (ATHOS-3 trial). Dopamine: limited use; more arrhythmias than NE (SOAP-II).

Assist-Control Volume Control (ACVC): most common ICU mode; set Vt/rate, patient can trigger extra breaths at full Vt; risk of breath stacking if tachypnea. Pressure Control (PC): set pressure limit, variable Vt (monitor for ↓Vt with worsening compliance). Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV): spontaneous breathing mode; patient determines rate/Vt; wean mode. CPAP: keeps airway open, no support for breathing effort (post-extubation, sleep apnea). Weaning: SBT (spontaneous breathing trial — PSV 5-8 cmH2O or T-piece × 30-120 min); pass = RR<35, SpO2>90%, no distress.

BSI occurring in patient with CVC not attributable to another source. Prevention bundle: hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier precautions during insertion, chlorhexidine skin prep, optimal catheter site selection (subclavian preferred over femoral), daily review of necessity and prompt removal. Treatment: vancomycin empirically (covers CoNS and MRSA). Remove catheter for S. aureus bacteremia (mandatory — re-culture at 48-72h, 14-day minimum therapy). Duration: 14 days for uncomplicated bacteremia; longer for persistent bacteremia/endocarditis.

Analgesia-first approach (PAD guidelines): treat pain before sedation (propofol, dexmedetomidine, midazolam). Propofol: GABA agonist, rapid onset/offset, choice for short-term sedation and neuro patients (daily wake-up allows neuro assessment); monitor TG, watch for PRIS. Dexmedetomidine (DEX): alpha-2 agonist, analgosedation, less delirium than midazolam (MIDEX/PRODEX trials), does NOT cause respiratory depression, can be used during extubation. Midazolam/lorazepam: benzodiazepines; increase delirium, accumulate in renal/hepatic failure; avoid when possible.

ADRENAL trial (2018): hydrocortisone 200mg/day → faster shock resolution but NO 90-day mortality benefit vs placebo. APROCCHSS trial (2018): hydrocortisone 200mg/day + fludrocortisone 50mcg/day → reduced 90-day mortality vs placebo in severe septic shock (MAP <65 despite NE ≥0.25 mcg/kg/min for ≥6h). Current recommendation (SSC 2021): consider hydrocortisone 200mg/day if vasopressor-refractory shock (NE ≥0.25 for ≥4h). Taper steroids as vasopressors wean. ACTH stimulation test not recommended for eligibility.
src: Annane et al. NEJM 2018 / Evans et al. 2021

def: Early enteral nutrition (EN) within 24-48h of ICU admission improves outcomes. PN (parenteral nutrition) only if GI tract non-functional or EN insufficient >7 days. Avoid overfeeding: target 25-30 kcal/kg/day; protein 1.2-2 g/kg/day (higher in critically ill). Refeeding syndrome: occurs in malnourished patients reintroduced to nutrition — hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia (thiamine before feeding). Gastric residual volumes >500 mL: consider prokinetics (metoclopramide, erythromycin). Thiamine 100mg before glucose in alcoholics (Wernicke prevention).

TRICC trial: restrictive transfusion (Hgb threshold 7 g/dL) non-inferior to liberal (9 g/dL) in stable ICU patients — reduces complications and cost. Exceptions: active MI/ACS (Hgb <8-10 transfuse), ongoing hemorrhage, symptomatic anemia. Platelet transfusion: prophylactic at <10,000/mcL (stable); <50,000 for procedures; <100,000 for neurosurgery/ophthalmology. FFP: NOT for prophylactic correction of elevated INR (no evidence); use for active bleeding + coagulopathy or prior to high-risk procedures with INR >1.5-2. TXA (tranexamic acid): within 3h of trauma reduces mortality (CRASH-2).

95% of patients labeled "penicillin allergic" can actually tolerate penicillin. True IgE-mediated reactions (immediate hypersensitivity within 1h): urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis. Cross-reactivity: penicillin → cephalosporin 1-2% (related to R1 side chain, not the ring); cephalosporin → carbapenem <1%. Approach: risk stratify, skin testing (PPL/MDM), graded oral challenge (direct challenge without skin test is now acceptable for most low-risk patients per ACAAI 2019). De-labeling saves costs, reduces use of broad-spectrum alternatives, reduces C. diff risk.

Monobactam antibiotic — unique beta-lactam containing only the monocyclic beta-lactam ring. Active ONLY against aerobic gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas); NO gram-positive or anaerobic coverage. Key clinical use: alternative to antipseudomonal agents in patients with severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy — aztreonam shares the same R1 side chain as ceftazidime (caution) but NOT cross-reactive with other penicillins/carbapenems. Useful for Pseudomonas coverage in penicillin/beta-lactam allergic patients.

Polymyxin B and colistin (polymyxin E) — last-resort agents for carbapenem-resistant organisms (KPC, NDM, OXA-48-producing CRE; carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter and Pseudomonas). Mechanism: disrupt gram-negative outer membrane (detergent-like). Major toxicity: nephrotoxicity (30-50%), neurotoxicity. Colistin requires conversion from prodrug colistimethate sodium (CMS) — calculate dose as colistin base activity (CBA). Inhaled colistin for VAP adjunctive therapy. Often combined with carbapenem or rifampin for synergy against pan-resistant organisms.

Azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin — inhibit 50S ribosomal subunit (23S rRNA). Used for: CAP (atypical coverage — Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella), MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin weekly), skin infections, H. pylori (clarithromycin triple therapy). Azithromycin: long intracellular t½ (68h — 5-day Z-pack = 10 days effect). QT prolongation risk (especially azithromycin + QT-prolonging drugs). Clarithromycin: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor — major drug interactions (statins → rhabdomyolysis; tacrolimus/cyclosporine → toxicity). Erythromycin: prokinetic effect (GI motility → used for gastroparesis).

Cell wall synthesis inhibitor (inhibits MurA enzyme in peptidoglycan synthesis pathway). Active against gram-positives and gram-negatives including MRSA and ESBL-producing organisms. Primary use: uncomplicated UTIs caused by E. coli or Enterococcus faecalis (single oral 3g dose). IV formulation (fosfomycin tromethamine) for complicated UTIs and MDR-gram negative infections (Europe, limited use in US). Poor CNS penetration. Must combine with other agents (resistance develops rapidly monotherapy). Resistance occurs via chromosomal mutations.

Nitroimidazole antibiotic with activity against anaerobes and certain protozoa. Forms toxic free radicals in anaerobic environments. Used for: C. difficile (mild only — fidaxomicin or vancomycin preferred for moderate/severe), BV, trichomoniasis, Giardia, Entamoeba, H. pylori (triple therapy), intra-abdominal infections (with gram-negative coverage). Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (nausea, flushing, tachycardia) — abstain from alcohol during and 48h after. Strong CYP2C9 inhibitor — significantly increases warfarin effect (check INR closely).

Glycylcycline (broad-spectrum tetracycline derivative) with activity against MDR gram-positives (MRSA, VRE), gram-negatives (ESBL, KPC — but NOT Pseudomonas or Proteus), and atypicals. NOT recommended for pneumonia (low serum levels) or BSI (black box warning: increased mortality vs comparators — use for specific indications only). Low serum/lung levels due to high Vd; good tissue penetration. Used for: complicated skin infections, complicated intra-abdominal infections, ESBL-producing organisms. Nausea is major adverse effect (30-40%).

Candidemia — treat ALL positive blood cultures (even without symptoms). Remove or replace all vascular catheters within 24h. Echinocandin (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) is first-line for moderate-severe illness or azole resistance risk. Fluconazole acceptable for less severe infections if C. glabrata and C. krusei ruled out (susceptibility available). Ophthalmology consult (hematogenous Candida endophthalmitis). Duration: 14 days from last positive culture AND clinical improvement. Repeat blood cultures every 24-48h until clear.

Opportunistic fungal infection in immunocompromised patients (HIV CD4 <200, solid organ transplant, prolonged high-dose steroids, rituximab). Presentation: subacute dyspnea, dry cough, low-grade fever; CXR — bilateral interstitial infiltrates; BAL with Gomori methenamine silver stain. LDH elevated. Treatment: TMP-SMX DS (15-20 mg/kg/day TMP divided q8h × 21 days) — drug of choice. Adjunct steroids if PaO2 <70 mmHg (reduce mortality). Alternatives: atovaquone, clindamycin + primaquine, pentamidine. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX SS daily when CD4 <200.

Structured community of microorganisms encased in a self-produced extracellular matrix attached to a surface (prosthetic valve, catheter, joint implant). Biofilm bacteria are 100-1000× more resistant to antibiotics than planktonic organisms due to limited drug penetration, altered metabolism, and persister cells. Clinical implications: prosthetic valve endocarditis, CRBSI, orthopedic implant infections typically require device removal for cure. Rifampin has unique anti-biofilm activity for staphylococci on prosthetics (rifampin + daptomycin/vancomycin for PJI).

Rapid onset or worsening of HF symptoms requiring urgent therapy. Hemodynamic profiles (Nohria-Stevenson): Cold/Wet (↓CO, ↑PCWP) = cardiogenic shock; Warm/Wet (normal CO, ↑PCWP) = most common — diuretics; Cold/Dry = low output without congestion — may need IV fluids + inotropes; Warm/Dry = compensated. IV loop diuretics (furosemide 1-2.5× oral dose IV); vasodilators (nitroglycerin for hypertensive HF, nitroprusside for severe); avoid BPs-lowering agents in cardiogenic shock. Ultrafiltration for diuretic resistance.

Narrow TI drug; therapeutic level 0.5-0.8 ng/mL for HF (lower than old targets). Toxicity symptoms: nausea/vomiting, visual changes (yellow-green halos, photopsia), bradyarrhythmias (AV block, junctional rhythm), accelerated junctional tachycardia, and bidirectional VT (pathognomonic). Risk factors: hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia potentiate toxicity — maintain K+ 4.0-5.5. Drug interactions: amiodarone, verapamil, clarithromycin, spironolactone (↑digoxin levels by 50-70%). Antidote: Digibind/DigiFab (digoxin-specific antibody fragments). Dose: #vials = (level × weight/100) or empirically 10 vials for severe toxicity.

Biventricular pacing to synchronize LV-RV contraction. Indications: LVEF ≤35%, LBBB with QRS ≥150ms (or 130-149ms with LBBB morphology), NYHA II-IV on GDMT. Improves symptoms, reduces hospitalizations, and reduces mortality (COMPANION, CARE-HF trials). CRT-D: combined CRT + ICD (preferred in most eligible patients). CRT-P: CRT without ICD (elderly, NYHA IV frail, or refusal). Requires electrophysiology implant. Non-LBBB with QRS ≥150ms: uncertain benefit. Narrow QRS: no benefit (EchoCRT trial).

Atherosclerotic plaque disease in coronary arteries. Stable CAD: angina with exertion, managed medically (aspirin, statin, beta-blocker, RAAS, sublingual NTG PRN). Unstable angina/NSTEMI: plaque rupture without complete occlusion (no ST elevation, troponin rise). STEMI: complete occlusion with ST elevation — primary PCI within 90 min (door-to-balloon); fibrinolysis if PCI unavailable within 120 min. Risk factor modification: statin (high-intensity for ASCVD), aspirin, smoking cessation, BP control <130/80, diabetes management.

High-intensity statins (reduce LDL ≥50%): rosuvastatin 20-40mg, atorvastatin 40-80mg. Moderate-intensity (30-49% LDL reduction): atorvastatin 10-20mg, rosuvastatin 5-10mg, simvastatin 20-40mg, pravastatin 40-80mg. Low-intensity (<30%): simvastatin 10mg, pravastatin 10-20mg. ACC/AHA 2018: high-intensity for clinical ASCVD, DM+ASCVD risk, LDL ≥190. Statin adverse effects: myopathy (dose-dependent), hepatotoxicity (rare), new-onset DM (small increased risk — benefit outweighs risk). CK monitoring only if symptomatic. Avoid simvastatin 80mg (rhabdomyolysis risk). CYP3A4 interactions: simvastatin + diltiazem, amiodarone, cyclosporine.

IMPROVE-IT trial: ezetimibe + simvastatin vs simvastatin alone after ACS → 32.7% vs 34.7% MACE at 7 years (HR 0.936, p=0.016; small but significant benefit; first non-statin to show incremental CV benefit).

Most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia. Chaotic atrial electrical activity → irregular ventricular response. Rate control: beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem/verapamil) for rapid ventricular response; digoxin + BB for sedentary patients. Rhythm control: amiodarone (most effective, most side effects), sotalol, flecainide (pill-in-pocket), dofetilide. Anticoagulation: CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (males) or ≥3 (females) → anticoagulate. DOACs preferred over warfarin. Cardioversion if >48h or unknown duration: anticoagulate 3 weeks before or TEE-guided (rule out thrombus).

Stroke risk stratification in AFib: C=CHF (+1), H=Hypertension (+1), A2=Age ≥75 (+2), D=Diabetes (+1), S2=Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism (+2), V=Vascular disease (+1), A=Age 65-74 (+1), Sc=Sex category female (+1). Total 0-9. Anticoagulation thresholds: males ≥2, females ≥3. Score 0 (male) or 1 (female): no anticoagulation recommended. Score 1 (male): may consider anticoagulation. Note: female sex is a risk modifier, not independent predictor — females with no other risk factors do not require anticoagulation.

After STEMI: Myoglobin rises first (1-4h), peaks 6-12h, normal by 24h (not cardiac-specific). CK-MB: rises 4-8h, peaks 12-24h, normal by 48-72h (can detect reinfarction). Troponin I/T: rises 3-6h (high sensitivity: 1-2h), peaks 24-48h, remains elevated 7-14 days. High-sensitivity troponin allows rapid rule-in/rule-out at 0h/1h (ESC protocol) or 0h/2h (ACC/AHA). Important: troponin elevation does not always mean Type 1 MI — consider Type 2 MI (supply-demand mismatch) in sepsis, PE, rapid AF, CKD.

Monoclonal antibodies (evolocumab = Repatha, alirocumab = Praluent) that inhibit PCSK9 enzyme, which normally degrades LDL receptors. Inhibition → more LDL receptors → marked LDL lowering (50-60% additional reduction). FOURIER trial (evolocumab): ↓ MACE 15% in established ASCVD on maximally tolerated statin, LDL reaching ~30 mg/dL. ODYSSEY OUTCOMES (alirocumab): similar benefit in post-ACS. Indication: ASCVD with LDL ≥70 on maximally tolerated statin + ezetimibe, or familial hypercholesterolemia. Subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks or monthly. Very expensive.

BNP (brain natriuretic peptide) released from ventricular myocytes in response to increased wall stress/stretch. Helps distinguish dyspnea from cardiac vs pulmonary causes in ED. Guides HF therapy: serial measurement allows assessment of treatment response and decongestion. NT-proBNP clearance depends on renal function — elevated in CKD without HF. Obesity reduces BNP levels (adipose tissue clears BNP). BNP-guided therapy shown to improve outcomes in outpatient HF (PRIMA trial). In-hospital BNP-guided therapy variable benefit.
src: ACC/AHA HF Guidelines / Maisel et al. NEJM 2002

def: Device implanted to detect and terminate life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (VF, VT). Primary prevention: LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months optimal GDMT + expected survival >1 year (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT). Secondary prevention: survivors of VF/hemodynamically significant VT not due to reversible cause. Programming: anti-tachycardia pacing (ATP) before shocks reduces inappropriate shocks. ICD placement avoidance: within 40 days post-MI (DINAMIT — no benefit), or within 3 months of new diagnosis (time for GDMT improvement of LVEF).

AV conduction disorders: First-degree: PR >200ms, no clinical significance. Second-degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach): progressive PR lengthening until dropped beat (AV node level — usually benign, can see with inferior MI, increased vagal tone). Mobitz II: sudden dropped beat without PR change (His-Purkinje level — serious, can progress to complete block, may need pacing). Third-degree (complete): no AV conduction, ventricular escape rhythm. Causes: inferior MI, digoxin toxicity, Lyme disease, cardiac surgery, medications (BB, CCB, amiodarone). Reversible causes should be treated first.

Severe hypertension (typically >180/120) with acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, papilledema, acute MI, aortic dissection, eclampsia, acute pulmonary edema, AKI). Requires immediate IV treatment. Choice depends on target organ: aortic dissection → esmolol + nitroprusside (reduce SBP to 120 within 20 min); hypertensive encephalopathy → labetalol or nicardipine; eclampsia → labetalol or hydralazine + magnesium. Goal: reduce MAP by no more than 25% in first hour (to prevent ischemia from sudden pressure drop). AVOID: immediate release nifedipine (sublingual — unpredictable BP drop).

KDIGO staging: Stage 1 — SCr increase ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR ≥1.5-1.9× baseline; UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6-12h. Stage 2 — SCr 2.0-2.9× baseline; UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥12h. Stage 3 — SCr ≥3× baseline OR ≥4.0 mg/dL OR initiation of RRT OR eGFR <35 in <18yo; UO <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24h or anuria ≥12h. Stages correlate with mortality. Key intervention: identify and treat underlying cause, avoid nephrotoxins, optimize hemodynamics, review all medications.

Functional renal failure in severe liver disease (cirrhosis + ascites) without intrinsic renal pathology. Type 1 HRS: rapidly progressive (SCr doubles to >2.5 mg/dL in <2 weeks) — poor prognosis. Type 2 HRS: slower progression, moderate renal failure, resistant ascites. Diagnosis of exclusion: no shock, nephrotoxin exposure, normal renal ultrasound, no improvement with albumin challenge. Treatment: norepinephrine or terlipressin + IV albumin 1 g/kg/day (CONFIRM trial: terlipressin). Liver transplantation is definitive treatment.
continuous IV infusion (no proven superiority over intermittent bolus in the DOSE trial, but often used empirically for refractory cases)

Bone disease from CKD-MBD (Chronic Kidney Disease-Mineral Bone Disorder): ↓eGFR → ↑phosphate (↓excretion) → ↓calcitriol (1,25-OH-D3) production → ↓Ca absorption → secondary hyperparathyroidism → bone resorption. Treatment: phosphate binders (sevelamer — preferred, non-calcium-based; calcium carbonate/acetate; lanthanum carbonate), active vitamin D analogs (calcitriol, paricalcitol — reduces PTH), cinacalcet (calcimimetic — for moderate-severe secondary HPT). Targets: phosphorus 3.5-5.5 mg/dL; Ca 8.5-10.5; PTH 2-9× upper normal (CKD 5D).

Normocytic normochromic anemia from reduced EPO production (peritubular cells). Absolute iron deficiency must be excluded/treated first (ferritin <100 or TSAT <20% → IV iron preferred in CKD). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs — epoetin alfa, darbepoetin alfa): indicated if Hgb <10 g/dL in non-dialysis CKD. Target Hgb 10-11.5 g/dL (higher targets increase CV events and mortality — TREAT trial warning). ESAs require adequate iron stores (ferritin 200-500, TSAT 20-50%). Avoid ESAs in active malignancy (thrombosis, tumor growth risk).

Physiological responses to maintain pH homeostasis. Metabolic acidosis: respiratory compensation (hyperventilation, ↓pCO2). Winter's formula: expected pCO2 = 1.5×(HCO3) + 8 ± 2. If measured pCO2 higher than predicted: concurrent respiratory acidosis. If lower: concurrent respiratory alkalosis. Metabolic alkalosis: hypoventilation, ↑pCO2 (limited by hypoxic drive). Respiratory acidosis: renal HCO3 retention (acute: +1 per 10 pCO2; chronic: +3.5). Respiratory alkalosis: HCO3 excretion (acute: -2; chronic: -5 per 10 pCO2). Delta-delta: checks for mixed anion gap + non-AG metabolic acidosis.

Nephrogenic DI: renal resistance to ADH (lithium toxicity, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, genetic) — urine osmolality remains low (little/no rise) after desmopressin challenge, in contrast to central DI where urine concentrates.

BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with weight-related comorbidity. FDA-approved pharmacotherapy: GLP-1 RA — semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly (Wegovy; STEP-1: 15% weight loss); tirzepatide 15mg weekly (Zepbound; SURMOUNT-1: 22% weight loss); liraglutide 3 mg daily (Saxenda; 8% weight loss). Phentermine/topiramate ER (Qsymia) — teratogenic, REMS required. Naltrexone/bupropion (Contrave) — CI with opioids, uncontrolled HTN. Orlistat (lipase inhibitor) — steatorrhea, fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption. Surgery: BMI ≥40, or ≥35 + comorbidities; most effective long-term (sleeve gastrectomy, RYGB).

Common endocrine disorder: Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3): oligovulation/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. Associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. Pharmacotherapy: OCPs for menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism; metformin for insulin resistance and ovulation induction; clomiphene or letrozole for fertility; spironolactone for hirsutism (anti-androgen). Weight loss (5-10%) improves menstrual function and metabolic markers. Screen for T2DM, dyslipidemia, hypertension.

Blood glucose <70 mg/dL (Level 1); <54 mg/dL (Level 2 = clinically significant); severe (requires assistance). Conscious patient: 15g fast-acting carbohydrates (4oz juice, glucose tablets) → recheck in 15 min; repeat if still <70. Unconscious/unable to swallow: D50W 25-50 mL IV push (preferred in hospital); glucagon 1 mg IM/SQ/IN (IM more reliable than nasal spray, use when no IV access); follow with complex carbohydrates after consciousness restored. Document and adjust regimen (if sulfonylurea-induced: may need prolonged monitoring; diazoxide for persistent sulfonylurea toxicity).
Anabolic therapy: teriparatide (recombinant PTH(1-34) analog), abaloparatide (PTHrP analog), romosozumab (sclerostin inhibitor).
Lecanemab (FDA-approved 2023) and donanemab (FDA-approved 2024) — anti-amyloid antibodies for early AD

Acute: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan) — 5-HT1B/1D agonists, first-line for moderate-severe; gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant) — CGRP receptor antagonists, especially if triptan contraindicated (CAD, hemiplegic migraine); lasmiditan (5-HT1F agonist, no vasoconstriction). NSAIDs + antiemetics for mild. Prophylaxis (≥4 attacks/month): topiramate, valproate, propranolol/metoprolol, amitriptyline (traditional); erenumab (anti-CGRP monoclonal antibody) — newer, highly effective. Ergotamines: largely replaced by triptans (more side effects, more contraindications). Medication overuse headache: limit acute medications <15 days/month.

Dopamine deficiency from substantia nigra degeneration → tremor (pill-rolling, rest), rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability. Treatment: levodopa/carbidopa (gold standard — carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion to dopamine, reducing nausea); dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole — use in younger patients to delay levodopa motor complications); MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline — modest benefit); COMT inhibitors (entacapone — extends levodopa effect). Wearing off: increase frequency of levodopa, add COMT inhibitor, or extended-release formulation. Dyskinesia (late complication): amantadine.

Warfarin is highly susceptible to drug and food interactions. CYP2C9 inhibitors (↑INR): fluconazole, amiodarone, metronidazole, trimethoprim-SMX, fluvoxamine, fluoxetine. CYP2C9 inducers (↓INR): rifampin (most potent, can reduce INR by 80%), carbamazepine, phenytoin, St. John's Wort, nafcillin. Vitamin K-containing foods: leafy greens (spinach, kale) — not contraindicated but must be consistent in intake. Herbals: garlic, ginkgo, fish oil (↑bleeding risk). Monitor INR 3-5 days after any change in perpetrator drug. Pharmacodynamic interactions: NSAIDs + warfarin = additive GI bleed risk.

Biguanide; first-line pharmacotherapy for T2DM. Mechanism: primarily reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis (AMPK activation); also improves peripheral insulin sensitivity. UKPDS: metformin reduces MI 39% in overweight patients — CV benefit beyond glucose lowering. HbA1c reduction: 1-1.5%. Weight neutral to slight weight loss. No hypoglycemia risk. Contraindications: eGFR <30 (lactic acidosis — small but real risk); hold 48h before iodinated contrast if eGFR 30-60 (restart when SCr stable). GI adverse effects (nausea, diarrhea) — take with food, start low 500mg, titrate. B12 deficiency with long-term use (monitor every 1-2 years).

Conversion table for opioid rotation: Morphine 10mg IV = Morphine 30mg PO = Hydromorphone 1.5mg IV = Hydromorphone 7.5mg PO = Oxycodone 20mg PO = Hydrocodone 20mg PO = Fentanyl 100mcg IV. Fentanyl patch (mcg/h) ≈ oral morphine equivalent (mg/day) / 2.4 (calculate MEDs then convert). When rotating opioids: reduce equianalgesic dose by 25-30% for incomplete cross-tolerance. MEDs (morphine equivalent daily dose): ≥90 MEDs associated with increased overdose risk. Methadone: complex conversion (non-linear, highly variable depending on prior opioid dose), requires specialist.

Universal with opioid therapy — unlike respiratory depression, tolerance does NOT develop to constipation. Management: stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) are first-line (do NOT rely on stool softeners alone — docusate insufficient). Add PEG (polyethylene glycol) for incomplete response. Peripherally-acting mu-opioid receptor antagonists (PAMORAs): methylnaltrexone (Relistor) SQ, naloxegol (Movantik), naldemedine (Symproic) — reverse GI opioid effects without crossing BBB (no reversal of analgesia). Alvimopan: hospital use only, for postoperative ileus prevention (limited supply).

Narrow TI: therapeutic range 0.6-1.2 mEq/L (acute therapy); 0.4-1.0 maintenance. Toxicity symptoms: tremor (fine early, coarse toxic), GI (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting), neurological (confusion, ataxia, dysarthria), seizures, cardiac arrhythmias. Risk factors: Na+ depletion (ACEi, thiazides — increase Li+ reabsorption), NSAIDs (↑levels 25-60%), dehydration. Therapeutic level does not rule out toxicity if chronic. Treatment: aggressive IVF with 0.9% NS (expand Na+ → less Li+ reabsorption), hemodialysis for severe toxicity (level >4 mEq/L or severe symptoms). Renally cleared; adjust with CrCl.

Clinically significant absorption interactions: 1. Chelation (divalent cations — Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+) with quinolones, tetracyclines (reduce absorption by 50-90%): separate by 2h before or 4-6h after. 2. Levothyroxine: calcium, iron, antacids, sucralfate, cholestyramine — take 4h apart. 3. Azole antifungals (itraconazole capsules, ketoconazole): require gastric acid for absorption — proton pump inhibitors reduce levels significantly. 4. Atazanavir, rilpivirine (HIV): need acidic stomach — PPIs contraindicated. 5. Mycophenolate: magnesium-containing antacids reduce Cmax by 40%.

X-linked enzyme deficiency affecting pentose phosphate pathway — reduces NADPH → reduced glutathione → oxidative hemolysis. Affects ~400 million people globally (highest prevalence in Africa, Mediterranean, Middle East, Asia). Triggers: rasburicase (absolute CI — causes hemolytic anemia; black box warning), primaquine, dapsone, methylene blue, nitrofurantoin, certain sulfonamides. Screen African American, Asian, and Mediterranean patients before rasburicase or high-dose dapsone. Hemolytic anemia with bite cells, Heinz bodies on peripheral smear.
src: G6PD Deficiency Guidelines / FDA Rasburicase Label

def: AGS Beers Criteria (2023) lists potentially inappropriate medications in adults ≥65. High-concern drugs: diphenhydramine (confusion, falls, urinary retention); tricyclic antidepressants (anticholinergic); benzodiazepines (falls, cognitive impairment, paradoxical agitation); non-BZD hypnotics/Z-drugs (zolpidem — falls, delirium); antipsychotics for dementia-related behavioral problems (increased mortality); NSAIDs (GI bleed, AKI, fluid retention, HTN); megestrol acetate (VTE, adrenal suppression). STOPP criteria (European equivalent). Goal: optimize medication regimen and deprescribe when appropriate.

Modified recombinant factor Xa decoy that binds and sequesters direct Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, fondaparinux). FDA-approved for life-threatening/uncontrolled bleeding with apixaban or rivaroxaban. ANNEXA-4 trial: hemostatic efficacy ~82%. Bolus + 2-hour infusion. Does NOT reverse dabigatran (DTI — use idarucizumab) or unfractionated heparin. Limitations: very expensive (>$25,000/dose), short half-life (1-2h — anti-Xa activity may rebound), thrombotic events post-reversal (restart anticoagulation as soon as safely possible). Available only in certified hospitals.

Humanized monoclonal antibody fragment that binds dabigatran with 350× higher affinity than thrombin, completely reversing its anticoagulant effect within minutes. FDA-approved for dabigatran reversal in emergent surgery/procedures or life-threatening bleeding. Dose: 5g IV (two 2.5g vials given 15 min apart). The RE-VERSE AD trial: successful reversal in 98% of cases. After reversal, dabigatran can be restarted 24h later. Does NOT reverse Xa inhibitors (use andexanet alfa) or warfarin (use 4F-PCC + vitamin K).

Direct thrombin inhibitor (DTI) that binds reversibly to thrombin active site. FDA-approved for HIT (alternative to heparin) and PCI in HIT patients. Hepatically metabolized — preferred in renal failure (unlike bivalirudin, which is primarily renally eliminated). Dose: 2 mcg/kg/min initially, titrate to aPTT 1.5-3× baseline. Important: argatroban INCREASES INR, making warfarin monitoring complex when transitioning (use chromogenic factor X assay or special nomograms). Hold argatroban when INR >4 and resume when confirmed warfarin effect adequate.

Most common nutritional deficiency worldwide. Lab findings: low MCV (microcytic), low ferritin (<30 mcg/L), low serum iron, elevated TIBC/transferrin, TSAT <20%. Symptoms: fatigue, pica, restless legs syndrome, pagophagia (ice craving). Treatment: oral iron (ferrous sulfate 325mg TID — take on empty stomach, with vitamin C for absorption; separate from calcium, antacids by 2h); IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose, low molecular weight dextran) for intolerance, CKD, IBD, HF, or non-response. In HF: IV iron (AFFIRM-AHF: ferric carboxymaltose ↓ HF hospitalizations).

Selective phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE-4) inhibitor that reduces cAMP degradation → ↓inflammatory cell activity in airways. Indicated as add-on maintenance therapy in COPD: FEV1 <50% + chronic bronchitis phenotype + ≥2 exacerbations/year. Reduces exacerbation rate by ~15-20% (as add-on to LABA/LAMA). NOT a bronchodilator — do not use for acute relief. ADRs: diarrhea, nausea, weight loss (1-2 kg average), psychiatric events (depression, suicidality — Black Box Warning). Avoid in active psychiatric conditions. Drug interaction: avoid with strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine).

Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD (risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure with high-flow O2 — removes hypoxic drive, Haldane effect). Otherwise target SpO2 94-98% for most patients. LTOT (long-term oxygen therapy) for COPD: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%, or PaO2 56-59 with cor pulmonale/polycythemia — improves survival (NOTT trial). Flow devices: nasal cannula 1-6 L/min; simple face mask 5-10 L/min; non-rebreather mask: 60-90% FiO2; HFNC: up to 60 L/min flow, 100% FiO2. Hyperoxia in ischemic stroke/MI/sepsis: potentially harmful (vasoconstriction, oxidative stress).

Mean PAP ≥25 mmHg (new definition ≥20 mmHg) with normal PCWP (<15). WHO Group 1. Pharmacotherapy: Endothelin receptor antagonists (ERA — ambrisentan, bosentan, macitentan): teratogenic, liver toxicity (bosentan); Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (PDE5i — sildenafil, tadalafil): block cGMP degradation; Prostacyclin analogs (epoprostenol IV, treprostinil SQ/inhaled, iloprost inhaled): epoprostenol is gold standard for severe PAH; Riociguat (sGC stimulator, NOT for PAH patients on PDE5i — hypotension). Triple combination preferred for high-risk patients. AVOID in decompensated LV failure (group 2).

Key spirometry parameter distinguishing obstructive from restrictive ventilatory defects. Normal: >0.70 (or >lower limit of normal). FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator = obstructive (COPD, asthma — reversible). FEV1/FVC >0.70 with ↓FVC and ↓TLC = restrictive (pulmonary fibrosis, obesity, chest wall disease). In COPD, GOLD staging is based on FEV1 % predicted: ≥80% = Grade 1; 50-79% = Grade 2; 30-49% = Grade 3; <30% = Grade 4. Asthma: ≥12% FEV1 improvement with bronchodilator (reversibility).

Complete Blood Count components: WBC (4.5-11 × 10^9/L) — differential reveals neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils; RBC — with Hgb (M: 13.5-17.5, F: 12-16 g/dL), Hct (M: 41-53%, F: 36-46%), MCV (80-100 fL), MCH, MCHC, RDW; Platelets (150-400 × 10^9/L). Anemia: Microcytic (MCV<80): iron deficiency, thalassemia, lead poisoning; Normocytic (80-100): anemia of chronic disease, AKI, hemolysis, blood loss; Macrocytic (MCV>100): B12/folate deficiency, medications (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, zidovudine), hypothyroidism.

PT (Prothrombin Time): extrinsic pathway (factors VII, II, V, X, fibrinogen). Prolonged by warfarin, liver disease, factor VII deficiency. Reported as INR for warfarin monitoring. aPTT (activated Partial Thromboplastin Time): intrinsic pathway (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII, prekallikrein) + common pathway. Used to monitor unfractionated heparin (target 60-100s or 1.5-2.5× normal). Anti-Xa level: preferred monitoring for LMWH, fondaparinux, and heparin in special populations. Thromboelastography (TEG/ROTEM): whole blood viscoelastic testing — guides transfusion in hemorrhage.

Fibrin degradation product indicating active clot formation AND lysis. High sensitivity (>95%) for VTE when abnormal but LOW specificity — elevated in: PE/DVT, DIC, surgery, trauma, cancer, pregnancy, infection, advanced age. Most useful as RULE-OUT test (negative D-dimer with low pre-test probability excludes VTE). Age-adjusted D-dimer threshold: (age × 10 mcg/L in patients >50) improves specificity. Elevated D-dimer is NOT diagnostic of PE/DVT without imaging confirmation. Also used in DIC diagnosis (elevated D-dimer + thrombocytopenia + prolonged PT/aPTT + low fibrinogen).

Normal serum ammonia: 15-45 mcmol/L (varies by lab). Elevated in: liver failure (hepatic encephalopathy), urea cycle disorders, portosystemic shunting, GI bleed in cirrhosis, valproate hepatotoxicity. Important caveat: ammonia level does NOT correlate well with degree of hepatic encephalopathy — clinical assessment (grading by West Haven criteria) is more important. Treatment is based on clinical grade, not ammonia level. Specimen handling: venous blood on ice to lab within 30 min (false elevation if not processed quickly).

Immunoassay-based screening test with class-level sensitivity but limited specificity (cross-reactivity causes false positives). Common false positives: diphenhydramine → TCA; quinolones, rifampin → opiates; ibuprofen → cannabinoid; venlafaxine → PCP; quetiapine → methadone (screen). Confirm positive results with GC-MS (gold standard). Window of detection: cannabinoids 3-30+ days (daily users); cocaine 2-4 days; opioids 2-4 days; benzodiazepines 3-7 days. Note: fentanyl and oxycodone are often NOT detected on standard opiate immunoassay screens.

Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events — composite endpoint used in cardiovascular outcome trials. Three-point MACE (3P-MACE): CV death, non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke. Four-point MACE (4P-MACE): adds hospitalization for unstable angina or HF. Used as primary endpoint in: EMPA-REG OUTCOME (empagliflozin), LEADER (liraglutide), FOURIER (evolocumab), PARADIGM-HF, and most major cardiovascular outcome trials. Composite endpoint allows more events to be captured, increasing statistical power but requiring careful interpretation of individual components.
src: FDA Cardiovascular Guidance / Multiple CV Outcome Trials

def: high risk (≥20%): high-intensity statin; intermediate (7.5 to <20%): moderate-intensity statin (consider high); borderline (5 to <7.5%): risk discussion/consider statin with enhancers; low (<5%): lifestyle.

For DRUG DOSING decisions, use Cockcroft-Gault equation (gives creatinine clearance in mL/min), NOT CKD-EPI or MDRD (which give eGFR in mL/min/1.73m²). Cockcroft-Gault: CrCl = [(140-age) × weight × 0.85 (if female)] / (72 × SCr). Use actual body weight (or IBW if obese, whichever is less). Critical in: renally-dosed antibiotics (vancomycin, aminoglycosides, pip-tazo), antiepileptics (levetiracetam, gabapentin), DOACs (dabigatran — contraindicated if CrCl <30; rivaroxaban — reduce for VTE if CrCl <30), metformin, digoxin.

Creatinine clearance estimated by Cockcroft-Gault: CrCl (mL/min) = [(140 - age) × weight (kg) × 0.85 if female] / [72 × SCr (mg/dL)]. Use actual body weight, unless obese — then use the lower of adjusted body weight (ABW = IBW + 0.4[TBW-IBW]) or IBW for renally-dosed drugs (avoid overestimating CrCl in obese). Important caveats: overestimates GFR in elderly with low muscle mass (seemingly normal SCr but reduced true function); underestimates in high-muscle bodybuilders.

Latin-derived dosing frequency abbreviations: QD/OD = once daily; BID = twice daily (every 12 hours); TID = three times daily (every 8 hours); QID = four times daily (every 6 hours); Q8H = every 8 hours; Q12H = every 12 hours; PRN = as needed; QHS = at bedtime; AC = before meals; PC = after meals. ISMP Dangerous Abbreviations: QD can be mistaken for QID (use "daily"); U (units) can be mistaken for 0 (use "units"); OD can mean once daily or right eye.
src: ISMP List of Confused Drug Abbreviations

def: Ideal Body Weight used for weight-based drug dosing to avoid overdosing in obese patients. Devine formula: Males: IBW = 50 + 2.3 × (height in inches - 60); Females: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 × (height in inches - 60). Adjusted Body Weight (AdjBW) = IBW + 0.4 × (actual weight - IBW) — used for renally-cleared drugs in obesity (aminoglycosides, vancomycin initial dosing, some heparin protocols). ARDS/ventilation: always use IBW for Vt calculation (6 mL/kg IBW), never actual weight. Aminoglycosides: use AdjBW if actual > 120% IBW.
src: Devine BJ, Drug Intell Clin Pharm 1974

def: Pro re nata (Latin: as needed). PRN medications are administered based on patient request or clinical indication, not on fixed schedule. Important context: scheduled medications are generally preferred for chronic conditions (better adherence, smoother drug levels). PRN use is appropriate for: breakthrough pain (oral opioids on top of scheduled basal analgesia), rescue bronchodilators (SABA PRN for asthma), antiemetics PRN for nausea, sleep medications, antidiarrheal agents. Overreliance on PRN pain medications (vs scheduled) is associated with undertreated pain in hospitalized patients.

Low Molecular Weight Heparin — enoxaparin (Lovenox), dalteparin, tinzaparin. Fragments of UFH with more predictable pharmacokinetics (anti-Xa > anti-IIa activity). Advantages over UFH: once- or twice-daily SQ dosing, no aPTT monitoring needed in most cases, less HIT risk (still possible — cross-reactive antibodies), outpatient use possible. Monitoring: anti-Xa level 4h post-dose if renally impaired, morbid obesity, or pregnancy. Treatment dose: enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SQ q12h or 1.5 mg/kg daily. Prophylaxis: 40mg SQ daily. Reduce in CrCl <30 mL/min (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg q24h for treatment).
Drug elimination rate is constant regardless of concentration (e.g., alcohol, phenytoin at high doses). Unlike first-order, doubling the dose more than doubles the drug effect and duration.
Elimination rate proportional to drug concentration — a constant fraction is eliminated per unit time. Most drugs follow this model. Half-life remains constant regardless of dose.

Saturable, nonlinear kinetics. At low concentrations follows first-order; at high concentrations becomes zero-order. Phenytoin and ethanol are classic examples. Small dose changes cause disproportionate level changes at high concentrations.

Large initial dose used to rapidly achieve therapeutic concentrations. LD = (Vd × Css) / F. Used when rapid attainment of steady-state is critical (e.g., digoxin, phenytoin, vancomycin in severe infections).
Dose given to maintain steady-state concentration. MD = CL × Css × τ / F. Determined by clearance and desired steady-state concentration, not volume of distribution.
Hypothetical volume that would contain total drug at plasma concentration. Vd = Dose/C0. High Vd (>1 L/kg) indicates extensive tissue distribution. Low Vd (<0.1 L/kg) suggests drug stays in plasma.
Fraction of drug that reaches systemic circulation unchanged. Oral F affected by gut wall metabolism and first-pass hepatic extraction. IV F = 100%. Key for dose conversion between routes.
Two formulations with AUC and Cmax within 80-125% of the reference product (90% CI). FDA standard for generic drug approval. Clinically relevant for narrow therapeutic index drugs (NTIs).
Used in PK/PD target attainment (AUC/MIC for vancomycin and fluoroquinolones; aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent, targeting Cmax/MIC).

Peak (Cmax) = highest concentration after a dose; Trough (Cmin) = lowest concentration before next dose. Traditional aminoglycoside monitoring strategy. Replaced by AUC-guided monitoring for vancomycin per 2020 ASHP guidelines.

Drug removed by kidneys via filtration, secretion, and reabsorption. CLR = (fe × CL). Affected by GFR, tubular secretion (OAT/OCT transporters), and urine pH. Dose adjust when CLR major elimination pathway.

Drug eliminated by liver = hepatic blood flow × extraction ratio (E). High-E drugs (>0.7): clearance flow-dependent — sensitive to cardiac output changes. Low-E drugs (<0.3): clearance capacity-dependent — sensitive to liver disease and protein binding.

Membrane proteins governing drug absorption, distribution, and elimination. P-glycoprotein (P-gp/MDR1) — efflux pump in gut/BBB/kidney. OAT/OCT — renal tubular secretion. OATP — hepatic uptake. Clinically relevant DDIs with P-gp inhibitors (amiodarone, verapamil) increasing drug levels.

ATP-dependent efflux transporter (MDR1/ABCB1). Pumps drugs out of cells — limits oral absorption and CNS penetration. Substrates: digoxin, dabigatran, colchicine. Inhibitors (amiodarone, clarithromycin) increase substrate levels. Inducers (rifampin) decrease levels.

Key polymorphisms: CYP2D6 (codeine → morphine conversion; ultra-rapid metabolizers at risk for morphine toxicity/respiratory depression, poor metabolizers lack analgesia), CYP2C19 (clopidogrel activation; poor metabolizers lack antiplatelet effect)
src: CPIC Guidelines; FDA Table of Pharmacogenomic Biomarkers

def: Measuring drug concentrations to optimize therapy. Indicated for: narrow therapeutic index drugs, variable pharmacokinetics, toxicity assessment. Examples: vancomycin (AUC-guided), aminoglycosides (extended-interval), phenytoin, digoxin, lithium, cyclosporine, tacrolimus.

Presystemic metabolism in gut wall or liver before reaching systemic circulation. Reduces oral bioavailability. High first-pass drugs include morphine, nitroglycerin, propranolol, lidocaine. Circumvented by IV, sublingual, transdermal, rectal routes.

Drug secreted into bile, hydrolyzed by gut bacteria, reabsorbed into portal circulation, prolonging effect and half-life. Relevant for: estrogen (oral contraceptive failure with antibiotics debated), mycophenolate, morphine, methotrexate. Cholestyramine can interrupt EHC as treatment strategy.

Pharmacologically inactive compound converted to active drug in vivo. Requires metabolic activation. Examples: clopidogrel→active thiol (CYP2C19), codeine→morphine (CYP2D6), enalapril→enalaprilat, valganciclovir→ganciclovir. Genetic polymorphisms affecting activation enzymes can alter efficacy/toxicity.

Agonist: binds receptor and activates it (produces response). Partial agonist: activates at less than full efficacy (e.g., buprenorphine). Antagonist: binds receptor without activation, blocks agonist. Competitive (reversible) vs. non-competitive (irreversible — aspirin COX, phenoxybenzamine). Inverse agonist: reduces baseline receptor activity.

Process of comparing patient's medication orders to all medications patient is taking to avoid medication errors such as omissions, duplications, dosing errors, and drug interactions. Required at all care transitions. Pharmacists play central role in BCPS practice.

Injury from a drug or its use — broader than ADR. Includes preventable (medication errors) and non-preventable events. ~3.5 million ER visits/year. Common causes: anticoagulants, insulin, opioids, antibiotics. ADE surveillance and pharmacist involvement reduce rates.

Noxious, unintended drug response at normal doses. WHO classification: Type A (augmented, dose-dependent, predictable — 80%): e.g., GI bleeds with NSAIDs. Type B (bizarre, idiosyncratic, dose-independent): e.g., anaphylaxis, SJS. Type C (chronic use). Type D (delayed). Type E (end of use). Type F (failure of therapy).

Immune-mediated ADR. Gell-Coombs classification: Type I (IgE-mediated/anaphylaxis — penicillin, NSAIDs), Type II (cytotoxic — drug-induced hemolytic anemia), Type III (immune complex — serum sickness), Type IV (T-cell mediated — contact dermatitis, SJS). Allergy assessment and de-labeling improves antibiotic stewardship.

Pharmacokinetic: absorption (chelation — antacids), distribution (protein displacement), metabolism (CYP inhibition/induction), excretion (renal competition). Pharmacodynamic: additive (aspirin+NSAID), synergistic (TMP-SMX), antagonistic (flumazenil+benzodiazepine), pharmacological overlap (QT prolongation with multiple drugs).

Macro-reentrant circuit in right atrium, typically 300 bpm with 2:1 conduction giving ventricular rate ~150 bpm. Classic sawtooth pattern in leads II, III, aVF. Stroke risk same as AFib — anticoagulate by same criteria. Cardioversion often easier than AFib. Catheter ablation (CTI ablation) highly effective (>95%).

Supraventricular tachycardia — includes AVNRT (most common), AVRT (accessory pathway), AT. Typically regular, narrow-complex, rate 150-250 bpm. Acute: vagal maneuvers → adenosine (6mg IV push, can repeat 12mg) → cardioversion if unstable. Adenosine contraindicated in WPW with pre-excitation. Chronic: AV nodal agents for AVNRT.

Wolff-Parkinson-White: accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) connecting atria and ventricles. ECG: short PR, delta wave, wide QRS. Risk of rapid conduction in AFib (potentially fatal). AVOID AV nodal blocking drugs (adenosine, beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin) in WPW with AFib — procainamide or cardioversion preferred. Definitive: catheter ablation.

Chaotic ventricular activity — no coordinated contraction — cardiac arrest. Immediate defibrillation (unsynchronized) required. ACLS: CPR → shock → epinephrine 1mg q3-5min → amiodarone 300mg or lidocaine → repeat shock. Survival decreases 10% per minute without CPR/defibrillation.

Biventricular pacemaker (±ICD = CRT-D) corrects ventricular dyssynchrony in HFrEF with LBBB. Indication: EF ≤35%, LBBB with QRS ≥150ms (Class I), QRS 120-149ms (Class IIa). Improves mortality, hospitalization, quality of life, and EF. Non-LBBB benefit uncertain.

Surgical revascularization preferred over PCI in: left main disease, 3-vessel CAD with reduced EF, complex multivessel disease (high SYNTAX score), diabetes with multivessel disease. LIMA to LAD graft preferred — superior long-term patency (>90% at 10 years vs. saphenous vein <50%).

ACS with troponin elevation without ST elevation. Represents ruptured plaque with partial coronary occlusion. Treatment: DAPT (aspirin+P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation (unfractionated heparin or enoxaparin), risk-stratify for coronary angiography timing. GRACE score determines early vs. ischemia-guided strategy.

ST-elevation MI: complete coronary occlusion. Immediate reperfusion: primary PCI preferred (<90 min door-to-balloon at PCI center, <120 min transfer). Fibrinolysis if PCI unavailable within 120 min. Medications: aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor (prasugrel or ticagrelor preferred), anticoagulation, high-intensity statin. Avoid NSAIDs post-MI.

Hemodynamic compromise from severe cardiac dysfunction: SBP <90 mmHg, CI <2.2 L/min/m², elevated filling pressures, end-organ hypoperfusion. Most common cause: STEMI. Treatment: emergent revascularization (PCI), vasopressors (norepinephrine preferred over dopamine per SOAP II), inotropes (dobutamine), consider mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella).
src: SOAP II Trial; AHA Scientific Statement on CS 2019

def: Pericardial inflammation. Symptoms: pleuritic chest pain (better leaning forward), friction rub, diffuse ST elevation (saddle-shaped), PR depression. Treatment: NSAIDs (ibuprofen or aspirin) + colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months (reduces recurrence per COPE/ICAP trials). Avoid anticoagulation in acute phase.

Fluid accumulation in pericardial space. Can develop slowly (large effusion, compensated) or rapidly (tamponade with small volume). Tamponade: Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds), pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg, electrical alternans on ECG. Immediate pericardiocentesis for tamponade.

Genetic (sarcomere mutations) myocardial hypertrophy, often asymmetric septal. LVOTO in 70%. Risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD). Avoid: digoxin, vasodilators, diuretics (preload reduction worsens obstruction), high-intensity exercise. Treatment: beta-blockers, disopyramide. Mavacamten (myosin inhibitor) approved 2022 for obstructive HCM. ICD for SCD prevention.

Enterobacteriaceae (Klebsiella, E. coli) resistant to all carbapenems. Mechanisms: KPC (carbapenemase, most common US), NDM, OXA-48, VIM. Treatment: ceftazidime-avibactam (KPC, OXA-48), meropenem-vaborbactam (KPC), imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam, cefiderocol. High mortality. Contact precautions essential.

Extended-spectrum beta-lactamase producing organisms (often E. coli, Klebsiella). Resistant to all penicillins and cephalosporins. Treatment: carbapenems (ertapenem for susceptible non-BSI; meropenem/imipenem for severe). Oral step-down: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or fluoroquinolone if susceptible (but resistance increasing). Avoid cephalosporins even if susceptible in vitro (inoculum effect).

S. aureus with mecA gene encoding PBP2a with low penicillin affinity. Resistance to all beta-lactams (except ceftaroline). Treatment: vancomycin (with AUC/MIC monitoring), daptomycin (not for pneumonia — inactivated by surfactant), linezolid, ceftaroline. MRSA decolonization (mupirocin nasal + CHG baths) for recurrent skin infections.

Most common hospital-acquired infection. Toxin A/B mediated colitis. Risk: recent antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins, carbapenems), PPIs, hospitalization, age. Treatment: fidaxomicin preferred (lower recurrence), vancomycin PO (not IV), metronidazole only for mild non-severe if cost barrier. Bezlotoxumab reduces recurrence. Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) for recurrent CDI.

Cystitis: dysuria, frequency, urgency — oral nitrofurantoin (5 days), TMP-SMX (3 days), or fosfomycin (1 dose). Pyelonephritis: oral fluoroquinolone (7 days) or TMP-SMX (14 days) if susceptible. Complicated UTI/urosepsis: IV ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone; adjust per culture. Asymptomatic bacteriuria: treat only in pregnancy or before urologic procedures.

UTI in patient with urinary catheter. Prevention: remove catheter ASAP, aseptic insertion, closed drainage system. Diagnosis requires symptoms — do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria (except pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure). Treatment same as complicated UTI. Duration: 7 days if prompt clinical response.

Infective endocarditis: infection of cardiac valves/endocardium. Modified Duke criteria for diagnosis. Common organisms: S. aureus (most common acute; IV drug use), viridans streptococci (subacute), enterococci, HACEK. Empiric: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (native valve); add gentamicin for prosthetic valve. 4-6 weeks therapy. Indications for surgery: heart failure, persistent bacteremia, abscess, prosthetic valve.

Opportunistic fungal pneumonia in immunocompromised (HIV CD4<200, transplant, prolonged steroids). Classic presentation: progressive dyspnea, dry cough, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, elevated LDH. Treatment: TMP-SMX (first-line), pentamidine (IV), atovaquone (mild-moderate), or TMP-dapsone for 21 days. Add prednisone if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS 3x/week.

Life-threatening meningitis in HIV (CD4<100) or other immunosuppression. Caused by Cryptococcus neoformans. Presents with subacute headache, fever, meningismus, elevated opening pressure on LP. Treatment: induction — liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine (2 weeks); consolidation — fluconazole 400mg (8 weeks); maintenance — fluconazole 200mg. Serial LP or lumbar drain for elevated ICP.

Active TB: RIPE regimen — Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol × 2 months (induction), then Rifampin + Isoniazid × 4 months (continuation). Add pyridoxine (B6) with INH to prevent neuropathy. Monitor LFTs. Latent TB: INH 9 months, rifampin 4 months, or weekly rifapentine+INH × 12 doses (3HP regimen). Directly observed therapy (DOT) reduces resistance.

Coordinated program to optimize antibiotic use, improving outcomes while reducing resistance and C. diff. Core strategies: prospective audit with feedback, formulary restriction with pre-authorization, de-escalation, IV-to-oral switch, optimal duration, culture-guided therapy. Pharmacists are central to stewardship teams. IDSA/SHEA core elements published 2016.

Sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors (MAP <65) and serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation. 30-day mortality ~40%. Norepinephrine first-line vasopressor. Bundle care: blood cultures × 2, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, early reassessment. Stress-dose steroids (hydrocortisone 200mg/day) if refractory shock.

Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome management centers on lung-protective ventilation: tidal volume 6 mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O. Severe ARDS: prone positioning ≥16 hours/day (PROSEVA trial — 16% vs 32% mortality). Paralytics (cisatracurium) in early severe ARDS: ACURASYS trial showed benefit, but ROSE trial did not. Conservative fluid strategy after resuscitation. No benefit for routine corticosteroids except methylprednisolone may help in late fibroproliferative phase.

AC/VC (Assist Control/Volume Control): set tidal volume, variable pressure — lung protective preferred. AC/PC (Pressure Control): set pressure, variable volume. SIMV: synchronized intermittent mandatory ventilation — rarely used. PSV (Pressure Support): partial support — used for weaning. CPAP: continuous positive pressure — spontaneous breathing. Key settings: FiO2, PEEP, rate, Vt, I:E ratio.

Spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) daily if: hemodynamically stable, FiO2 ≤50%, PEEP ≤8 cmH2O, adequate mental status, minimal secretions. SBT: T-piece or low-level PSV for 30-120 min. If tolerated → extubate. RSBI (RR/Vt) <105 predicts success. Sedation vacation (SAT) combined with SBT (PAC trial) reduces ICU days and mortality.

Acute brain dysfunction — hypo/hyperactive/mixed — in 60-80% of mechanically ventilated patients. Assessed by CAM-ICU or ICDSC. Risk: sedation (especially benzodiazepines), pain, immobility, sleep disruption, infection. ABCDEF bundle: Awakening, Breathing, Choice of analgesia/sedation, Delirium assessment, Early mobility, Family. Antipsychotics (haloperidol, quetiapine) lack RCT evidence for prevention/treatment.

Evidence-based ICU care bundle: A=Assess/manage pain; B=Spontaneous awakening+breathing trials; C=Choice of sedation/analgesia (minimize benzodiazepines); D=Delirium monitoring/management; E=Early mobility and exercise; F=Family engagement. Implementation associated with 68% reduction in ICU delirium, shorter ventilation, reduced mortality.
src: Ely et al., Critical Care Medicine 2001; SCCM PADIS 2018

def: Post-cardiac arrest neuroprotection. TTM Trial (2013): 33°C vs 36°C — no difference in mortality or neurologic outcome. TTM2 Trial (2021): 33°C vs normothermia (prevent fever) — no benefit of hypothermia. Current AHA 2020: prevent fever (≥37.5°C) for 72h, either 33-36°C or normothermia acceptable. Avoid hyperthermia.

Prevention of stress-related mucosal disease (SRMD) in ICU. Indications: mechanical ventilation >48h, coagulopathy (platelets <50k, INR >1.5, PTT >2×ULN). PPIs and H2 blockers comparable efficacy (SUP-ICU: no difference in GI bleeding). PPIs increase C. diff and pneumonia risk. Limit to high-risk patients; discontinue on discharge.

KDIGO CKD classification: G1 (GFR ≥90), G2 (60-89), G3a (45-59), G3b (30-44), G4 (15-29), G5 (<15 or dialysis). Albuminuria categories: A1 (<30 mg/g), A2 (30-300), A3 (>300). Combination of GFR and albuminuria determines risk. Progression: manage BP, RAAS inhibition, glycemic control, avoid nephrotoxins.

Extracorporeal blood purification — diffusion across semipermeable membrane. Three times weekly typically. Removes small to medium molecules (BUN, creatinine, K+, drugs). Drug removal by HD: small, water-soluble, low-protein-bound, low-Vd. Supplement dose post-HD for renally-cleared drugs. Access: AV fistula preferred, AV graft, tunneled catheter.

Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy — for hemodynamically unstable AKI. Modes: CVVH (convection), CVVHD (diffusion), CVVHDF (convection+diffusion). Slower, continuous ultrafiltration/dialysis. Drug removal: varies by mode, effluent rate, filter membrane, protein binding. Generally remove more drug than intermittent HD at typical effluent rates of 20-25 mL/kg/h. Consult pharmacokinetics references for dosing.

K+ >5.5 mEq/L (severe >6.5). ECG changes: peaked T waves → PR prolongation → wide QRS → sine wave. Treatment stepwise: (1) Stabilize membrane: calcium gluconate 1g IV if ECG changes; (2) Shift: insulin 10U+D50, albuterol 10-20mg nebulized, sodium bicarbonate (if acidosis); (3) Eliminate: sodium polystyrene, patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate, furosemide, dialysis. Avoid kayexalate in post-op bowel surgery patients (necrosis risk).

Acute kidney injury after iodinated contrast (rise in Cr ≥0.5 mg/dL or 25% within 48-72h). Risk: pre-existing CKD (eGFR<30 highest risk), DM, heart failure, high contrast volume. Prevention: IV hydration (isotonic saline) before and after — most effective intervention. N-acetylcysteine: evidence inconsistent; some guidelines still recommend. Hold metformin if eGFR<30 or 48h post-procedure.
src: KDIGO AKI Guidelines; ACR Contrast Manual

def: CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault using actual body weight for obese patients) guides most dosing adjustments. Key drugs: antibiotics (β-lactams, aminoglycosides, vancomycin), anticoagulants (LMWH: avoid if CrCl<30; use UFH), antivirals (acyclovir, ganciclovir), antidiabetics (metformin: hold if eGFR<30; contrast; SGLT2i: eGFR threshold for efficacy), digoxin, gabapentin. Dialyzable drugs: supplement post-HD.
src: ASHP Drug Prescribing in Renal Failure; Aronoff et al.

def: Autoimmune destruction of beta cells. Absolute insulin requirement. Management: basal-bolus insulin (MDI) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/pump). Carb counting for meal doses. Target A1C <7% for most. ADA standards: CGM-based metrics (TIR >70%, TBR <4%). DKA treatment: IV fluids, insulin drip, K+ replacement, address precipitant.

Diabetic ketoacidosis: hyperglycemia (usually >250), ketoacidosis (pH<7.30, bicarb<15), anion gap >12. Precipitants: infection, missed insulin, new T1DM. Treatment: IV normal saline resuscitation → replace K+ (if <3.5, add KCl before insulin) → insulin drip 0.1 U/kg/hr → transition to D5W when glucose <250 → close anion gap → transition to subcutaneous insulin.

TSH elevated, free T4 low. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardia, depression, hair loss, delayed DTRs. Treatment: levothyroxine (T4) — start 1.6 mcg/kg/day. Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks. Drug interactions: antacids, calcium, iron reduce absorption (take 4h apart or 30-60 min before food). Myxedema coma: IV T4 200-400mcg then 75-100mcg daily + stress-dose steroids.

TSH low, free T4/T3 elevated. Causes: Graves (most common — diffuse goiter, exophthalmos, pretibial myxedema), toxic nodule, thyroiditis. Treatment: beta-blockers for symptoms, thionamides (methimazole preferred, PTU in first trimester pregnancy/thyroid storm), radioactive iodine (RAI), or surgery. Thyroid storm: PTU + saturated solution potassium iodide (SSKI, 1h after PTU) + beta-blocker + glucocorticoids + cooling.

Primary (Addison's disease): adrenal cortex destruction — low cortisol+aldosterone, high ACTH, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hyperpigmentation. Secondary: pituitary/hypothalamic → low ACTH, low cortisol only. Adrenal crisis: hypotension, nausea, abdominal pain → hydrocortisone 100mg IV bolus then 50-100mg q6-8h + aggressive fluids. Chronic: hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg early PM; fludrocortisone for primary AI.

Primary: adenoma (most common) → elevated Ca++, low-normal phosphate, elevated PTH. Symptoms: bones, groans, moans, stones (renal calculi). Hypercalcemia management: IV saline → furosemide, bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid), calcitonin, denosumab, dialysis for severe cases. Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) for secondary HPT in CKD/ESRD. Surgery for primary HPT with symptomatic disease.

Na+ <135 mEq/L. Classify by tonicity, volume status, and urine Na/Osm. Euvolemic hyponatremia (SIADH): fluid restriction ± salt tabs, tolvaptan (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist) for severe/refractory. Hypovolemic: isotonic saline. Hypervolemic (HF, cirrhosis, ESRD): fluid restriction, loop diuretics, tolvaptan. Correction rate: no faster than 8-10 mEq/L in 24h (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome). 3% saline for severe symptomatic (seizure, coma): 1-2 mL/kg/h bolus.

Seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery. Treatment: benzodiazepines (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or diazepam 0.15 mg/kg — first-line; IM midazolam 10mg pre-hospital). Second-line (after 2 benzo doses): fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV, valproic acid 40 mg/kg IV, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (ESETT trial: all three second-line agents equally effective). Third-line (refractory): phenobarbital, propofol, midazolam, ketamine infusions.
Time-sensitive treatment: IV alteplase (tPA, 0.9 mg/kg, max 90mg — 10% bolus, 90% over 1hr) OR tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg, max 25mg, single bolus) within 4.5h

Secondary prevention after ischemic stroke/TIA: antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81mg, clopidogrel, or aspirin+extended-release dipyridamole). Cardioembolic stroke (AFib): anticoagulation — DOACs preferred over warfarin. Aspirin + clopidogrel for 21 days after minor stroke/TIA (POINT/CHANCE trials), then monotherapy. Aggressive BP control (<130/80), statin therapy, carotid endarterectomy if stenosis ≥70%.

Autoimmune disorder — acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies (85%) block neuromuscular junction. Symptoms: fatigable proximal muscle weakness, ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia. Myasthenic crisis: respiratory failure requiring intubation. Precipitants: infection, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, neuromuscular blockers. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), prednisone, azathioprine, IVIG, plasmapheresis (crisis), eculizumab/efgartigimod for refractory AChR+.

Demyelinating CNS disease — relapsing-remitting (RRMS) most common. Acute exacerbation: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3-5 days. Disease-modifying therapy (DMT): high-efficacy (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab, cladribine) vs. moderate-efficacy (interferons, glatiramer, teriflunomide, dimethyl fumarate). Treatment decisions balance efficacy vs. risk (PML with natalizumab — check JC virus antibody). Siponimod/ofatumumab for secondary progressive MS.

Simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage from systemic activation of coagulation. Triggers: sepsis, trauma, malignancy, obstetric catastrophe. Labs: low platelets, elevated PT/INR/PTT, elevated D-dimer, low fibrinogen. Treatment: treat underlying cause, replace components (FFP, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen<100, platelets for active bleeding), heparin for thrombosis-dominant DIC.

Immune-mediated thrombocytopenia from IgG antibodies against heparin-PF4 complexes. Platelet drop >50% (usually to 20-100k), 5-14 days post-heparin. 4T score for probability. STOP all heparin (including flushes, LMWH, heparin-coated catheters). Start non-heparin anticoagulant: argatroban (liver clearance — preferred in renal failure), bivalirudin (direct thrombin inhibitors). HIT antibody (PF4 ELISA), confirm with SRA. Can transition to warfarin after platelet recovery.

PRBC threshold: hemoglobin <7 g/dL (stable ICU patients, TRICC trial); <8 g/dL post-cardiac surgery or symptomatic CAD; <10 g/dL rarely. 1 unit PRBC raises Hgb ~1 g/dL. Platelet transfusion: <10k prophylactic; <50k for procedures/surgery; <100k for CNS/ophthalmologic surgery. FFP: for active bleeding with elevated INR >1.5 or prior to procedures. Cryoprecipitate: fibrinogen <100-150 mg/dL.

Preventive: hydroxyurea (increases HbF, reduces crises), crizanlizumab (P-selectin inhibitor), L-glutamine. (Note: voxelotor/Oxbryta was withdrawn from worldwide markets in Sept 2024 for safety concerns and is no longer available.)

GOLD classification by spirometry + symptoms. Stepwise treatment: SABA PRN → LAMA (tiotropium, umeclidinium) or LABA → LAMA+LABA → add ICS (triple therapy). ICS: reduce exacerbations in frequent exacerbators with blood eosinophils ≥300. Exacerbation: short-course steroids (5 days prednisone 40mg, equivalent to 14-day course), antibiotics (if purulent sputum or hospitalized), SABA±SAMA nebs.

GINA step approach: Step 1-2: low-dose ICS-formoterol PRN (preferred over SABA). Step 3: low-dose ICS-LABA. Step 4: medium-dose ICS-LABA ± tiotropium. Step 5: high-dose ICS-LABA + biologics. Biologics (severe eosinophilic asthma): mepolizumab, benralizumab (IL-5/5Rα), dupilumab (IL-4/13), omalizumab (IgE). Add-on options: LAMA, theophylline, montelukast (black box: neuropsychiatric).

Risk stratification: massive (hemodynamic instability) → systemic thrombolysis (tPA 100mg over 2h) or surgical/catheter embolectomy + anticoagulation. Submassive (RV strain/troponin rise): anticoagulation, consider CDT. Low-risk: LMWH or DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban — no bridging needed). Duration: provoked 3 months; unprovoked ≥3 months with reassessment; cancer-associated: LMWH or edoxaban/rivaroxaban.

Progressive fibrosing interstitial lung disease. Honeycombing on HRCT. Anti-fibrotic therapy slows decline: nintedanib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) or pirfenidone (anti-fibrotic, anti-inflammatory). Both reduce annual FVC decline by ~50%. Monitor LFTs with both agents. Lung transplant for eligible patients. Avoid immunosuppression (may increase mortality per PANTHER-IPF).

Systematic approach: (1) pH — acidosis <7.35, alkalosis >7.45; (2) Primary disorder: PaCO2 (respiratory) vs. HCO3 (metabolic); (3) Compensation present? (4) Delta-delta ratio (AG acidosis: check for mixed disorder). Normal: pH 7.35-7.45, PaCO2 35-45, HCO3 22-26, PaO2 80-100, SaO2 95-100%. Respiratory acidosis: hypoventilation (COPD exacerbation, opioid OD). Metabolic acidosis: MUDPILES mnemonic.

WBC: 4.5-11k (left shift: bands >10% → infection/inflammation; >30k: leukemoid reaction or CML). Hgb: 13.5-17.5 (M), 12-15.5 (F). MCV: <80 microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease); 80-100 normocytic; >100 macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, liver disease, medications — MTX, hydroxyurea, zidovudine). Platelets: 150-400k.

ALT/AST: hepatocellular injury (ratio >2:1 favors alcoholic hepatitis). ALP/GGT: cholestatic pattern. Total bilirubin: >2.5 clinically jaundiced. Direct (conjugated) bilirubin elevated in cholestasis/hepatocellular disease. Indirect (unconjugated) elevated in hemolysis/Gilbert syndrome. Albumin: marker of synthetic function (decreased in cirrhosis/malnutrition). PT/INR: synthetic function marker.

TSH: most sensitive screen (0.4-4.0 mIU/L). Free T4: active hormone (0.8-1.8 ng/dL). Free T3: most active (2.3-4.2 pg/mL). Overt hypothyroidism: TSH elevated + low free T4. Subclinical hypothyroidism: TSH elevated + normal free T4. Overt hyperthyroidism: TSH suppressed + elevated free T4/T3. Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies: Hashimoto thyroiditis. TSI/TRAb: Graves disease.

BUN: 7-20 mg/dL. Creatinine: 0.6-1.2 (F), 0.7-1.3 (M) mg/dL. BUN/Cr ratio: >20 suggests prerenal; <10 suggests intrinsic renal or post-renal. Cystatin C: alternative GFR marker (not affected by muscle mass like creatinine). Urine albumin-creatinine ratio (UACR): microalbuminuria 30-300 mg/g, macroalbuminuria >300 mg/g. CKD-EPI equation for eGFR calculation.

Hypomagnesemia (<1.7 mg/dL): causes hypocalcemia and hypokalemia (refractory if Mg not replaced). Hypophosphatemia (<2.5 mg/dL): refeeding syndrome, DKA treatment, causes respiratory muscle weakness. Hypocalcemia (<8.5 mg/dL): hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, critical illness — treat with IV calcium gluconate if symptomatic/severe. Hypercalcemia (>10.5): HHM, primary HPT, malignancy.

Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme inhibitor — drug class that blocks conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Examples: lisinopril, enalapril, captopril, ramipril. Indications: HFrEF, post-MI, DM nephropathy, hypertension, CKD with proteinuria. Key SE: dry cough (10%), angioedema (switch to ARB — shared mechanism in bradykinin pathway). Contraindications: pregnancy, bilateral RAS.
Less angioedema risk (cross-reactivity exists but low — roughly 3-10%; meta-analysis ~3.5% for confirmed angioedema, ~9.4% for any angioedema).

International Normalized Ratio — standardized prothrombin time ratio. Normal: ~1.0. Therapeutic anticoagulation (warfarin): INR 2-3 for most indications; 2.5-3.5 for mechanical mitral valve. Supratherapeutic INR management: hold warfarin (INR 3-5), vitamin K oral/IV (INR>10, active bleeding), 4-factor PCC (urgent reversal), FFP (if PCC unavailable). INR unreliable measure for coagulopathy in liver disease.

NIH Stroke Scale — 15-item neurological examination quantifying stroke severity. Scores: 0=no stroke, 1-4=minor, 5-15=moderate, 16-20=moderate-severe, 21-42=severe. Guides thrombolysis decisions and prognosis. Assessed at baseline and at intervals. Hemorrhagic transformation more common with higher NIHSS scores post-tPA.

Mean Arterial Pressure = DBP + (1/3)(SBP-DBP) or (SBP+2×DBP)/3. Normal: 70-105 mmHg. Minimum goal in septic shock: MAP ≥65 mmHg (higher goals in chronic HTN patients: ≥80). MAP ≥70 in cardiogenic shock. MAP ≥50 acceptable in some hemorrhagic shock during damage control. End-organ perfusion pressure depends on MAP.

Area Under the Curve to Minimum Inhibitory Concentration ratio — primary PK/PD target for concentration-dependent antibiotics. Vancomycin: AUC/MIC 400-600 mg·h/L (for MIC ≤1) per 2020 ASHP/IDSA/SIDP guidelines. Aminoglycosides: AUC/MIC >80-100 for gram-negatives. Azithromycin: tissue AUC/MIC relevant for atypical pathogens.

Number Needed to Treat (NNT) = 1/ARR (absolute risk reduction) — how many patients need treatment to prevent 1 additional outcome. Lower NNT = more effective therapy. Number Needed to Harm (NNH) = 1/ARI — how many patients treated before 1 additional adverse event occurs. Favorable when NNT<NNH. Used to communicate clinical significance of trial results.
